notes.title: 当FIFA遇到美国：世界杯，太赚了！
notes.excerpt: 这期视频用2026年美加墨世界杯为案例，拆解FIFA如何把世界杯从一个主要靠门票收入的赛事，发展成由转播权、赞助、门票、招待业务、授权和主办城市权益共同驱动的全球体育商业机器。核心论点是：世界杯这个超级IP遇到美国成熟的体育消费和赛事商业化体系后，收入被推到新高，但也把票价、转售、广告暂停、无障碍观赛成本和球迷体验的争议一起放大了。 世界杯为什么越来越贵？
notes.html:
当FIFA遇到美国：世界杯，太赚了！

What This Is About

这期视频用2026年美加墨世界杯为案例，拆解FIFA如何把世界杯从一个主要靠门票收入的赛事，发展成由转播权、赞助、门票、招待业务、授权和主办城市权益共同驱动的全球体育商业机器。核心论点是：世界杯这个超级IP遇到美国成熟的体育消费和赛事商业化体系后，收入被推到新高，但也把票价、转售、广告暂停、无障碍观赛成本和球迷体验的争议一起放大了。

Important Concepts

  • 赛事-媒体-品牌三角：阿迪达斯、可口可乐和早期体育营销人把世界杯包装成品牌全球传播的平台，让赛事内容、电视传播和商业赞助相互放大。
  • 赞助权益分层：FIFA从单一顶级赞助模式，扩展到FIFA合作伙伴、世界杯合作伙伴、区域赞助商等层级，降低部分品牌进入门槛，同时扩大总收入。
  • 转播权价值重估：卫星电视、私营媒体和体育内容竞争，让世界杯版权从早期打包低价，跃升为数十亿美元级别的核心收入来源。
  • 美式体育商业化：1984年洛杉矶奥运会、1994年美国世界杯、NFL/NBA/MLB的票务与赞助实践，为2026年世界杯的动态定价、转售佣金、停车费、招待业务和广告时间提供了模板。
  • 非营利组织的商业张力：FIFA名义上是非营利机构，但其储备金、赞助收入、门票定价和权益售卖已经接近大型商业娱乐公司的运营逻辑。

Learning Takeaways

  • 世界杯的商业化不是单点创新，而是几十年里围绕IP扩军、媒体分发、赞助权益、主办地选择和票务机制逐步叠加的结果。
  • 美国市场给FIFA带来更高的消费能力和体育商业基础设施，也让世界杯更容易采用动态定价、官方转售佣金、主办城市赞助等高变现做法。
  • 商业收入增长会改变产品本身：补水暂停可能兼具球员保护和广告库存功能，决赛中场演出也可能改变足球比赛原有的节奏和仪式感。
  • 票价和无障碍座位政策说明，体育IP越稀缺，越需要关注商业最大化和公共可及性之间的边界。
  • 主办城市和国家并非只能承担成本；通过税收谈判、城市赞助权益和本地消费，它们也会争取从FIFA的全球IP中切出自己的收益。

Questions To Explore

  • FIFA作为非营利组织，应不应该对世界杯门票设置可及性或无障碍票价底线？
  • 动态定价和不限价转售会提高资源配置效率，还是会把核心球迷排除在现场体验之外？
  • 足球比赛引入固定补水暂停和决赛中场演出后，商业价值与比赛完整性之间的边界在哪里？
  • 2030年百年世界杯回到多个历史源头举办，是否会修正2026年过度商业化带来的球迷反弹？

Useful Examples/Tools/Links

  • 2026年世界杯：48支球队、104场比赛、三国16城举办，是FIFA扩大曝光和收入的关键变量。
  • 1984年洛杉矶奥运会：独家转播招标、行业独家赞助和高价门票，被视频作为现代奥运盈利模式的代表案例。
  • 1994年美国世界杯：USA94权益包、纪念品、广告曝光量化和高上座纪录，展示美国对足球赛事商业化的早期改造。
  • 可口可乐、阿迪达斯、吉列、富士、百威、麦当劳：视频中用于说明世界杯赞助体系成型的早期品牌案例。
  • StubHub、SeatGeek、FIFA官方转售平台：观察高价门票是否真实成交，以及转售价格如何回落的渠道。

Optional Post Draft

世界杯为什么越来越贵？

这期《硅谷101》的重点不是足球战术，而是FIFA怎么把世界杯变成全球最会赚钱的体育IP。门票、转播、赞助、招待业务、停车、转售佣金、城市赞助，全都被拆成可以变现的模块。2026年美加墨世界杯尤其典型：美国成熟的体育消费市场给了FIFA更大胆的定价空间，也带来了动态定价、官方转售、补水暂停广告等争议。

最值得想的是：当一项运动的商业化能力强到可以改变票价、观赛门槛和比赛节奏时，它还是原来那个属于球迷的世界杯吗？

notes.title: Midjourney breaks into ... healthcare? #AI #Medtech #MidjourneyMedical #Medicalbreakthrough
notes.channel: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
notes.excerpt: This short clip says Midjourney is using its highly profitable AI-image business to fund a move into preventative medical imaging. The speaker frames the project as a new ultrasoun
notes.html:
Midjourney breaks into ... healthcare? #AI #Medtech #MidjourneyMedical #Medicalbreakthrough

What This Is About

This short clip says Midjourney is using its highly profitable AI-image business to fund a move into preventative medical imaging. The speaker frames the project as a new ultrasound-like device that could make internal body scans cheaper, faster, and more approachable than MRI-style care.

Important Concepts

  • Profitable AI labs as funders: The clip argues that a small, high-revenue AI company can reinvest cash into unrelated or adjacent deep-tech bets.
  • Preventative medical imaging: The goal described is not only diagnosis after symptoms, but routine scanning that could catch issues earlier.
  • Ultrasound vs. MRI positioning: The device is described as cheaper, faster, and more scalable than MRI, though the clip does not provide clinical validation details.
  • Consumerized care experience: The speaker says the scan should feel closer to a spa visit, implying a lower-friction patient experience.

Learning Takeaways

  • AI companies with strong margins may increasingly use their cash flow to enter capital-intensive sectors such as healthcare hardware.
  • The biggest claim is not just a new imaging device, but a scale ambition: the clip mentions a goal of one billion scans per year.
  • Medical imaging claims need extra scrutiny; the transcript gives a business and product narrative, not evidence on accuracy, approvals, safety, or clinical outcomes.
  • If the device works as described, the strategic wedge is accessibility: faster and cheaper scans could shift imaging from scarce specialist capacity toward routine prevention.

Questions To Explore

  • What exactly is Midjourney’s medical imaging entity, and is it directly owned by Midjourney or a separate venture by the same founders/team?
  • What regulatory approvals would this device need before broad clinical use?
  • How does its image quality, false positive rate, and diagnostic utility compare with standard ultrasound, CT, and MRI?
  • What are the risks of making frequent whole-body scans feel casual or spa-like?

Useful Examples/Tools/Links

  • Transcript claim: Midjourney has around 40 employees and about $200 million in revenue.
  • Product category to research: preventative ultrasound-based medical imaging.
  • Benchmark comparisons to check: MRI cost, scan time, availability, and clinical use cases.
  • Validation sources to look for next: FDA filings, peer-reviewed studies, clinical trial records, and radiology expert reviews.

Optional Post Draft

Midjourney moving into healthcare is interesting less because “AI company does medicine,” and more because profitable AI labs now have cash to make hardware bets. The clip claims they are building a cheaper, faster ultrasound-style scanning device for preventative care, with a huge scale target.

The open question: is this a real clinical breakthrough, or a compelling product story that still needs regulatory and medical proof?

notes.title: Nobody’s ready for Claude Fable 5 #Anthropic #AI #Fable5 #Claude
notes.channel: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
notes.excerpt: This very short clip is a reaction to using Claude Fable 5. The speaker’s main point is that the model felt unusually capable: the limiting factor was no longer the model failing,
notes.html:
Nobody’s ready for Claude Fable 5 #Anthropic #AI #Fable5 #Claude

What This Is About

This very short clip is a reaction to using Claude Fable 5. The speaker’s main point is that the model felt unusually capable: the limiting factor was no longer the model failing, but the user running out of sufficiently ambitious tasks to give it.

Important Concepts

  • Capability ceiling vs. task ceiling: The clip contrasts a model hitting its limits with a user struggling to invent bigger, more meaningful asks.
  • User bottleneck: As models improve, the scarce skill may shift toward framing large tasks, delegating clearly, and knowing what outcomes are worth asking for.
  • Ambition gap: The comment implies that users may underuse frontier models if they keep giving them small, familiar prompts.

Learning Takeaways

  • A useful way to evaluate an advanced model is to ask whether it expands the size and complexity of work you are willing to delegate.
  • Better models may require better task design: bigger goals, clearer constraints, and more willingness to let the model work across multiple steps.
  • The transcript is only a single-sentence testimonial, so it should be treated as a subjective signal rather than a benchmark.

Questions To Explore

  • What concrete tasks made the speaker feel Fable 5 had not hit its limit?
  • How does Fable 5 perform on long-horizon work compared with Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, and Codex-style agents?
  • What prompt patterns help users move from “small asks” to high-leverage delegation?
  • Where does the model still fail when tasks require judgment, external validation, or real-world execution?

Useful Examples/Tools/Links

  • Evaluation idea: compare models on the same open-ended project brief, then inspect planning quality, execution depth, and recovery from ambiguity.
  • Personal workflow test: list the ten largest tasks you avoid delegating to AI, then try rewriting one as a constrained project brief.
  • Useful metric: whether the model increases the scope of work you can responsibly hand off, not just whether it answers a prompt well.

Optional Post Draft

The most interesting line in this clip: the limit was not the model running out of ability, but the user running out of big things to ask for.

That may be the next AI skill gap. Not “how do I prompt this small task,” but “what work am I willing to delegate now that the model can handle more context, ambiguity, and follow-through?”

notes.title: AI didn't make you faster. It just hid the real bottleneck. #Productivity #FutureOfWork
notes.channel: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
notes.excerpt: AI tools can make implementation dramatically faster, but that does not automatically make the whole organization faster. The transcript argues that once building features gets eas
notes.html:
AI didn't make you faster. It just hid the real bottleneck. #Productivity #FutureOfWork

What This Is About

AI tools can make implementation dramatically faster, but that does not automatically make the whole organization faster. The transcript argues that once building features gets easier, the bottleneck shifts downstream or upstream into meetings, PRDs, approvals, coordination, and decision-making habits that were designed for a slower production cycle.

Important Concepts

  • Moving bottlenecks: removing one constraint in a system usually exposes the next constraint rather than eliminating constraints entirely.
  • Workflow inertia: teams often keep old planning and approval rituals even after AI changes the speed of prototyping and shipping.
  • Build-versus-decide gap: the feature may be faster to build than to discuss, scope, or approve.
  • Learning through shipping: when prototypes are cheap, testing multiple versions can become more useful than debating one perfect plan.

Learning Takeaways

  • AI productivity should be measured at the system level, not just by how quickly one person can write code or produce artifacts.
  • If meetings, PRDs, and approvals take longer than implementation, the organization has not captured the full value of faster tools.
  • Teams may need to redesign decision loops, feedback cycles, and ownership boundaries around the new speed of execution.
  • The useful question is not only "Can AI make this task faster?" but "Where does the bottleneck move after this task is faster?"

Questions To Explore

  • Which parts of our workflow now take longer than actually building the thing?
  • What decisions could be made through small shipped experiments instead of extended planning?
  • How should planning quality be maintained when prototypes are cheap and fast?
  • What metrics reveal whether AI is improving end-to-end cycle time?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • Manufacturing bottleneck theory as a lens for AI-era knowledge work.
  • PRD-to-prototype comparisons: track whether documentation, approval, or implementation is the slowest step.
  • Experiment portfolios: ship several small versions and use real feedback to choose direction.

Optional Post Draft

AI did not remove the bottleneck. It moved it.

When a prototype takes less time than the meeting about the prototype, the constraint is no longer implementation. It is decision-making, coordination, and old process habits. The next productivity gain may come from redesigning how teams choose, test, and learn.

notes.file: 2026-06-27-对话王熙乔-ai时代的教育者-十年沉浮-与人类文明的下一步.md
notes.title: 对话王熙乔：AI时代的教育者、十年沉浮，与人类文明的下一步
notes.excerpt: This is a long interview with Wang Xiqiao, founder of 探月学校, about ten years of experimenting with AI-era education. The conversation connects his early prediction that routine work
notes.html:
对话王熙乔：AI时代的教育者、十年沉浮，与人类文明的下一步

What This Is About

This is a long interview with Wang Xiqiao, founder of 探月学校, about ten years of experimenting with AI-era education. The conversation connects his early prediction that routine work would be replaced by AI with 探月's mission: cultivating "inner abundance" and "active citizenship" rather than optimizing only for grades, credentials, or narrow job preparation.

Important Concepts

  • AI-era education: if routine, procedural work is automated, schools need to train creativity, adaptability, curiosity, judgment, and the capacity to live meaningfully.
  • 探月's learner goal: "内心丰盈的个体，积极行动的公民" means students should be inwardly grounded and outwardly responsible.
  • Values: "善及万物、探求真理、坚毅笃行" condenses care, truth-seeking, and resilient action.
  • School as future-society simulator: 探月 treats school as a place to test new social, technological, and educational forms before they are mainstream.
  • Felt love and happiness as trainable capacities: Wang argues that sensing love, giving love, and experiencing happiness are not vague traits but abilities that can be practiced.
  • Frontier education economics: early experiments can be expensive and niche, but validated methods may later become more broadly accessible.

Learning Takeaways

  • The interview reframes AI anxiety as an educational design problem: what should humans cultivate when machines can do more tasks?
  • Wang's answer is not anti-technology; he sees technology as a tool and an amplifier of human nature, so the quality of human nature matters more.
  • 探月's story shows both the promise and fragility of experimental education: campus moves, regulatory pressure, funding questions, and parent trust all shaped the school.
  • The strongest through-line is that education is not only skill transfer. It is formation of perception, purpose, relationships, courage, and action.
  • For parents and educators, the practical anchor is to model the life stance they hope children develop, not merely explain it.

Questions To Explore

  • How can schools assess "inner abundance" and "active citizenship" without reducing them to another rigid rubric?
  • Which parts of 探月's model can scale beyond affluent families and boutique school settings?
  • What does a curriculum for sensing love, giving love, and building happiness actually look like day to day?
  • How should traditional academic rigor coexist with AI-native creation, entrepreneurship, and personal formation?
  • If technology amplifies human nature, what habits should schools build before giving students more powerful tools?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • 探月学校's mission phrase: "内心丰盈的个体，积极行动的公民".
  • The school's values: "善及万物、探求真理、坚毅笃行".
  • Student innovation formats mentioned in the interview: hackathons, high-school incubator work, AI-assisted creation, and entrepreneurship-oriented projects.
  • Classical references used by Wang: Socratic inquiry into happiness and self-knowledge; 《中庸》 on "天命之谓性，率性之谓道，修道之谓教".
  • Wang's 2025 essay themes: intelligence and automation, environmental energy, life sciences, augmented/virtual reality, and space exploration as civilization-shaping technologies.

Optional Post Draft

AI时代的教育，不只是教孩子赢过机器。

王熙乔和探月学校给出的答案是：培养内心丰盈、能积极行动的人。因为当技术越来越强，真正决定文明方向的不是工具本身，而是使用工具的人有没有感知幸福、付出爱、探求真理和笃定行动的能力。

notes.title: This is the real AI moat — and it's not the models. #anthropic #claude #claudecowork
notes.channel: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
notes.excerpt: This short clip argues that Anthropic's real advantage is not only model quality, but the operating speed of an AI-native organization. The example is Claude Co-work: Anthropic rep
notes.html:
This is the real AI moat — and it's not the models. #anthropic #claude #claudecowork

What This Is About

This short clip argues that Anthropic's real advantage is not only model quality, but the operating speed of an AI-native organization. The example is Claude Co-work: Anthropic reportedly noticed developers using an internal coding tool to organize expense receipts, then built and shipped a productized version in about 10 days.

Important Concepts

  • Operational velocity can become a moat when teams can observe unexpected internal use, decide quickly, and ship fast.
  • AI-native companies may gain leverage from using their own tools in unconventional workflows before customers ask for them explicitly.
  • Product discovery can come from watching how capable users bend tools toward adjacent problems.
  • The "moat" is framed as a system of fast sensing and fast execution, not just a better foundation model.

Learning Takeaways

  • Treat surprising internal use cases as product signals, especially when users apply a tool outside its original category.
  • Speed matters most when paired with tight feedback loops; the 10-day timeline is the point of the example.
  • Competitive advantage in AI may increasingly come from organizational habits: dogfooding, rapid prototyping, and fast shipping.
  • When evaluating AI companies, look beyond benchmark claims and ask how quickly their teams convert observed behavior into working products.

Questions To Explore

  • What internal workflows are currently being solved with the "wrong" tool because the right product does not exist yet?
  • How can a product team systematically capture accidental or emergent uses without overfitting to anecdotes?
  • What organizational structures let an AI team safely ship a new workflow in days instead of quarters?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • Example: developers using a coding tool to organize expense receipts, which became a signal for Claude Co-work.
  • Tool referenced: Claude Co-work.
  • Useful lens: evaluate AI companies by product-cycle speed, not only model releases.

Optional Post Draft

The interesting part of Claude Co-work is not "expense receipts." It is the 10-day response time.

The clip's argument: Anthropic noticed developers using a coding tool for receipt organization, then quickly turned that behavior into a product direction. That kind of operational velocity may be the real AI moat.

Model quality still matters. But in AI, the teams that win may be the ones that notice weird user behavior first, dogfood aggressively, and ship before the insight goes stale.

notes.title: You're learning AI wrong. Here's the fix #AI #Management #Leadership #FutureOfWork
notes.channel: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
notes.excerpt: This short clip reframes AI fluency as a management discipline rather than a narrow prompting skill. The point is that people who get strong results from AI act less like tool oper
notes.html:
You're learning AI wrong. Here's the fix #AI #Management #Leadership #FutureOfWork

What This Is About

This short clip reframes AI fluency as a management discipline rather than a narrow prompting skill. The point is that people who get strong results from AI act less like tool operators and more like managers, teachers, and editors: they define the work, guide the process, inspect output, and give feedback.

Important Concepts

  • AI as a management skill: the valuable skill is directing work, not memorizing prompt tricks.
  • Managerial judgment: good AI use depends on setting goals, clarifying expectations, and knowing what quality looks like.
  • Teaching as prompting: useful interaction with AI resembles explaining a task to a person who needs context, examples, and feedback.
  • People skills as AI skills: delegation, critique, iteration, and communication become more important as models become more capable.

Learning Takeaways

  • Training for AI should include delegation, review, and feedback loops, not only prompt templates.
  • Better users will be the ones who can decide what should be done and evaluate whether the output is actually useful.
  • If AI is treated like a worker or collaborator, the user's role shifts toward scoping, coaching, and quality control.
  • The durable advantage is not a specific model feature; it is the ability to manage intelligent systems toward a clear outcome.

Questions To Explore

  • What management habits translate most directly into better AI workflows?
  • How should teams teach AI delegation to people who have never managed other people?
  • What review standards are needed before AI output can be trusted in high-stakes work?
  • How does this framing change the design of internal AI training programs?

Useful Examples/Tools/Links

  • Ethan Mollick's framing that strong AI users often behave like good managers and teachers.
  • Use AI practice tasks that require goal-setting, critique, revision, and final acceptance criteria.
  • Build personal checklists for reviewing AI output: accuracy, completeness, audience fit, and next action.

Optional Post Draft

Most people are learning AI like it is a software tool.

This clip argues the better frame is management.

The best AI users are not just good at prompts. They are good at defining work, giving context, checking quality, and iterating. In other words: delegation, teaching, and judgment.

The skill to build next is not "perfect prompts." It is learning how to manage an intelligent system toward a useful result.

notes.title: Verso, l'entreprise qui ne dort jamais
notes.excerpt: Verso's co-founder explains how the company built an AI-native consumer research business and an autonomous internal operating system around a "Company Brain." The talk is less abo
notes.html:
Verso, l'entreprise qui ne dort jamais

What This Is About

Verso's co-founder explains how the company built an AI-native consumer research business and an autonomous internal operating system around a "Company Brain." The talk is less about using AI inside the product alone and more about designing the company, workflows, and product surfaces so agents can listen, think, and act across the business.

Important Concepts

  • AI-native organization: Verso was designed from day one for agents to operate core workflows, not just assist employees.
  • Company Brain: an internal system that listens to company data, thinks through intelligence plus memory, and acts through tools, MCPs, CLIs, and product access.
  • Context provider: Verso gives clients near real-time consumer context for product, messaging, and visual decisions.
  • End-to-end research automation: study design, respondent recruitment, AI interviews, translation, analysis, and delivery are orchestrated as one workflow.
  • Agent-controllable product design: the product itself was built so AI agents can interact with it directly.
  • Human escalation: the Brain handles routine execution and pings people for judgment, fraud review, or exceptional cases.

Learning Takeaways

  • The largest gains come when AI is embedded into the operating model, not added as a thin productivity layer.
  • Autonomy depends on context quality: emails, calendars, Slack, Notion, client knowledge, and methodology need to be accessible and retrievable.
  • Designing software for agents changes the product requirements; interfaces and workflows must be controllable by non-human operators.
  • Verso compresses traditional six-week research delivery into roughly seventy-two hours by automating coordination and analysis.
  • The team claims leverage from automation across customer success, bug triage/fixing, recruiting, and daily executive digests.

Questions To Explore

  • What governance is needed when a Company Brain can act across client workflows and internal systems?
  • How does Verso measure the quality and bias risk of AI-moderated consumer interviews?
  • Which actions should always require human approval, even if the agent has enough context to proceed?
  • How can companies retrofit agent access into products that were not designed for AI control from day one?
  • What memory architecture prevents stale or incorrect organizational context from driving actions?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • OpenAI Voice models for AI-moderated consumer interviews.
  • Realtime Translation for multilingual research workflows.
  • Frontier-model multi-agent analysis pipeline for research synthesis.
  • Codex automations for collecting and orchestrating company context.
  • Integrations mentioned: emails, calendars, Slack, Notion, tools, MCPs, CLIs, and the Verso application itself.

Optional Post Draft

Verso's lesson: don't just give every employee an AI assistant. Redesign the company so AI can operate the workflow.

Their "Company Brain" listens to company context, retrieves memory, and acts through tools. That is how a four-person team can deliver research studies in days instead of weeks.

notes.title: I Was The Only Thing Connecting Claude, ChatGPT, and Codex. So I Built My Replacement.
notes.channel: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
notes.excerpt: Nate B. Jones introduces "Open Engine," a framework for coordinating work across Claude, Codex, ChatGPT, OpenClaw, Hermes, humans, and teams without waiting for native integrations
notes.html:
I Was The Only Thing Connecting Claude, ChatGPT, and Codex. So I Built My Replacement.

What This Is About

Nate B. Jones introduces "Open Engine," a framework for coordinating work across Claude, Codex, ChatGPT, OpenClaw, Hermes, humans, and teams without waiting for native integrations. The core idea is to move from isolated chats to a shared queue where agents and people can claim work, carry context, leave receipts, and hand tasks off transparently.

Important Concepts

  • The human-as-hallway problem: when every agent lives in its own room, people become the copy-paste layer between tools.
  • Queue as system of record: Linear, Jira, Kanban, or another agent-readable queue can hold owner, status, context, sources, limits, and definition of done.
  • Prompt mode vs work mode: a prompt asks for an answer; a ticket assigns a result that another person or agent can review and build on.
  • Receipts and audit trails: agents should claim tasks, show what they did, say where they stopped, and move status visibly.
  • Boundary problem: the bottleneck is not only agent intelligence, but how work moves from one model, tool, teammate, or household context to another.
  • Human judgment remains in the loop: agents should stop for exact blocking questions when ambiguity, permission, or approval is required.

Learning Takeaways

  • Better agents can still create more management work if there is no shared state layer.
  • A ticket is a better coordination object than a chat transcript because it can contain context, sources, ownership, status, and completion criteria.
  • Multi-agent workflows need explicit handoff protocols: who owns it, what context matters, what the agent can do, where it must stop, and what proof it should leave.
  • The goal is not one agent that replaces everything. It is a clean coordination layer that lets preferred tools do the work they are best at.
  • Household and team workflows have the same underlying shape when changes cascade across calendars, messages, projects, approvals, and follow-up tasks.
  • Useful autonomy means agents carry work until human judgment is actually needed, instead of repeatedly asking humans to relay state between systems.

Questions To Explore

  • What queue should act as the shared system of record for my own agents: Linear, Jira, Notion, GitHub Issues, or something custom?
  • What labels, statuses, and receipt format would make agent work auditable without adding too much overhead?
  • Which workflows currently force me to copy context between Claude, Codex, ChatGPT, Slack, email, and docs?
  • How should agents claim-lock work so two agents do not duplicate or conflict with each other?
  • What should count as "needs input" versus safe autonomous execution?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • Open Engine: Nate's framework for queue-based agent coordination.
  • Open Brain: referenced as the earlier memory layer; Open Engine is positioned as the movement/handoff layer.
  • Linear queue: Nate's preferred queue example, though he says Jira, Kanban, or custom systems can also work.
  • Example loop: request -> issue -> claim -> work -> proof -> receipt -> next item.
  • Example delegation: Maya asks Codex to route a metrics spec to Leo's Claude agent through a self-contained Linear issue.
  • Example work-mode task: classify a support ticket, attach customer history, escalate only if rules are met, and show where the agent stopped.

Optional Post Draft

The big idea in Open Engine: stop being the hallway between your AI tools.

If Claude, Codex, ChatGPT, Slack, and your team's agents all produce useful fragments, the hidden work becomes handoff labor. You copy context. You explain status. You remember what changed.

Nate's proposed fix is simple: put the work in a queue that humans and agents can both read.

A good agent ticket should include:

  • owner
  • context and sources
  • allowed actions
  • stop conditions
  • definition of done
  • receipt of what happened

That changes the workflow from "ask an AI for an answer" to "assign work that another person or agent can review and build on."

notes.title: Builders Unscripted: Ep. 4 - Pietro Schirano
notes.excerpt: Roman from OpenAI interviews Pietro Schirano, a designer, engineer, founder, musician, and early model tester. The conversation centers on how Pietro evaluates frontier models, how
notes.html:
Builders Unscripted: Ep. 4 - Pietro Schirano

What This Is About

Roman from OpenAI interviews Pietro Schirano, a designer, engineer, founder, musician, and early model tester. The conversation centers on how Pietro evaluates frontier models, how Codex and GPT-5.5 change creative software building, and how AI shifts work from direct execution toward supervision, direction, and rapid customer feedback.

Important Concepts

  • Personal evals: Pietro tests new models with recurring tasks around instruction following, execution quality, multimodal reasoning, agentic behavior, and creative direction.
  • Multimodal creativity: one demo converts an image into music and reconstructs the image from the sound, combining vision, audio, cryptography-like encoding, and aesthetic judgment.
  • Codex as an everyday assistant: Pietro uses Codex not only for coding, but also for file organization, music metadata cleanup, notes, dashboards, and local-system workflows.
  • Agent supervision: as models become more capable, the human role shifts toward describing goals, reviewing outputs, and managing long-running agents.
  • Product velocity: MagicPath uses Codex and GPT-5.5 to prototype features quickly, shorten feedback loops, and ship more customer-informed iterations.
  • AI as a translation layer: AI helps PMs, designers, engineers, and founders move across disciplines by translating intent into working artifacts.

Learning Takeaways

  • Build your own model eval set from tasks you actually care about; generic benchmarks miss taste, workflow fit, and creative edge cases.
  • A model upgrade is most meaningful when it makes previously brittle workflows "just work," especially vision-to-code, agent delegation, and multimodal tasks.
  • Strong agents benefit from explicit persistence instructions: complete the goal, review for regressions, surface blockers, and do not stop early.
  • Coding is becoming more like a malleable material: a fast way to capture an idea into a working interface, game, dashboard, or device experiment.
  • Faster building does not remove product judgment. It increases the need to choose what not to build and to keep customers close.
  • The durable value may be saved human time: agents can run while people step away, then return with finished work, receipts, or requests for review.

Questions To Explore

  • What personal evals would reveal whether a new model is actually better for my workflows?
  • Which "drawer ideas" are newly feasible because the model can now handle design, code, audio, vision, and local automation together?
  • How should teams manage the flood of possible features when AI makes building much cheaper?
  • What does good supervision look like when agents can run for 20-30 minutes or longer without close monitoring?
  • How can product teams give every role access to agents without creating fragmented context across tools?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • Codex shortcuts: Pietro uses short triggers such as "Spawn" for multi-agent work and review prompts for bug/regression checks.
  • Image-to-sound app: GPT-5.5 and Codex generate a system that turns the OpenAI logo into music and reconstructs it from the audio.
  • Odysseus writing app: an autocomplete writing tool with real-time voice input and text continuation.
  • Build Web Apps plugin: described as combining GPT image generation for design with GPT-5.5 implementation.
  • Hardware hacking demo: Codex reads a connected device, understands its capabilities, and builds a Doodle Jump-style game plus a work notification tool.
  • MagicPath: Pietro's infinite canvas for humans and agents to design and build together.

Optional Post Draft

Pietro Schirano's model-testing process is a useful template:

1. Keep personal evals, not just benchmark vibes.

2. Test instruction following, execution quality, multimodal reasoning, and agent delegation.

3. Try the ideas that used to feel too weird or too hard.

4. Use agents for everyday computer work, not only coding.

5. Let customer feedback, not feature excitement, decide what survives.

The strongest line of the interview: coding is becoming like clay. The constraint is less "can I build this?" and more "is this the thing worth building?"

notes.title: AI时代“超级高中生” 、大学外的第三种可能与中国创新教育｜探访北京探月学校
notes.excerpt: This long-form visit to Beijing's Moonshot Academy explores whether AI-era education needs a path beyond exam preparation and university admissions. The school positions itself as
notes.html:
AI时代“超级高中生” 、大学外的第三种可能与中国创新教育｜探访北京探月学校

What This Is About

This long-form visit to Beijing's Moonshot Academy explores whether AI-era education needs a path beyond exam preparation and university admissions. The school positions itself as an experimental K12 learning community that emphasizes self-knowledge, project work, entrepreneurship, social-emotional learning, inclusive education, and transferable competencies such as judgment, creativity, collaboration, and agency.

Important Concepts

  • "A third possibility": Moonshot Academy is testing a path where high school students can build real projects, companies, or careers instead of treating university admission as the only exit.
  • One Small Step program: a high school entrepreneurship track where students use AI and technology to identify problems, prototype, pitch, iterate, and graduate through external financing, stable income, or commercial transfer.
  • Competency-based education: the school emphasizes transferable abilities such as critical thinking, information literacy, creativity, judgment, aesthetics, self-direction, and habits of mind.
  • Real-world project learning: students work through design studios, maker spaces, capstone projects, exhibitions, social-impact work, and product development rather than only abstract assignments.
  • Rebuilt school relationships: teachers are framed as "guardians," students as "learners," and mentorship, coaching, and house systems support cross-age community life.
  • AI as a pressure test: the transcript argues that when factual knowledge is easier to access, human judgment, question selection, self-understanding, and values become more important.
  • Whole-child support: social-emotional learning, life skills, sex education, wayfinding, and inclusive education are treated as core parts of schooling rather than side programs.
  • Limits of the model: the school is expensive, hard to scale, and best suited to students and families who can tolerate ambiguity, autonomy, and delayed proof of value.

Learning Takeaways

  • AI-era education may need to shift from "learn more facts" toward "decide what is worth asking, making, and verifying."
  • Project-based learning works best when it has real stakes: users, audiences, investors, public exhibitions, community problems, or actual customers.
  • The school does not reject academics; it teaches math, science, humanities, AP courses, and philosophy, but tries to connect them to history, civilization, design, and lived problems.
  • Autonomy is not automatically liberating. Students need self-discipline, agency, mentorship, and emotional tools to handle freedom productively.
  • The most interesting part of the model is its through-line: from elementary SEL and integrated projects, to middle-school STREAM and life skills, to high-school capstones and entrepreneurship.
  • The school's strongest claim is not that everyone should skip university; it is that university should become one option among several credible paths.
  • Innovation education has a cost structure problem: small classes, mentors, makerspaces, rich projects, and individualized support are difficult to make broadly affordable.

Questions To Explore

  • Which parts of Moonshot's model could public schools adopt without reproducing the full private-school cost structure?
  • How should schools assess competencies like judgment, curiosity, agency, and collaboration without reducing them to shallow rubrics?
  • What kinds of students struggle in highly autonomous project-based environments, and what scaffolding helps them succeed?
  • How should universities and employers evaluate applicants whose strongest evidence is projects, companies, or public work instead of grades?
  • When AI handles factual recall and first drafts, what foundational knowledge still needs to be taught directly and systematically?

Useful Examples/Tools/Links

  • Moonshot Academy / Beijing Tan Yue School as the central case study.
  • "One Small Step" entrepreneurship pathway with AI hackathons, industry feedback, financing, employment, or commercialization as exits.
  • Student examples: a smart wake-up bedside lamp, a design studio serving classmates, and an assistive reading product for children with dyslexia.
  • Course examples: Locus of My Point in math, Monopoly as an economics simulation, design exhibitions, maker-space prototypes, stop-motion STREAM projects, and life-skill modules.
  • Frameworks referenced in the transcript: PBL, STREAM, SEL, NGSS, capstone projects, system thinking, self-awareness, Eastern and Western philosophy, and inclusive education.

Optional Post Draft

AI时代的教育，可能不是多加几节AI课这么简单。

这期探访北京探月学校，最有价值的不是"高中生创业"这个热闹标签，而是它提出了一个更难的问题：当知识获取越来越便宜，学校到底还应该训练什么？

探月的答案是素养：判断力、审美、好奇心、自驱力、协作、表达、理解自己和真实解决问题。

它的做法也很激进：高中生做真实项目、创业、办展览、用MakerSpace造东西；小学和初中就开始做社会情感学习、跨学科项目、生活技能和AI素养。

但它也不是一个轻松答案。高成本、难复制、强依赖学生自驱力，也要求家庭接受不确定性。

我觉得它最值得学习的一点是：大学不是被否定，而是从"唯一出口"变成了"多种人生路径之一"。

notes.title: What if plants could talk?
notes.excerpt: This playful OpenAI short imagines a houseplant that can directly tell a person what it needs: blinds opened, humidity increased, less shade, more light, or water. The clip uses hu
notes.html:
What if plants could talk?

What This Is About

This playful OpenAI short imagines a houseplant that can directly tell a person what it needs: blinds opened, humidity increased, less shade, more light, or water. The clip uses humor to show how AI-style interfaces could make hidden needs easier to understand and act on.

Important Concepts

  • Ambient communication: the fantasy is not just a talking plant, but an environment that can surface needs in plain language.
  • Sensor-to-action loop: plant conditions such as light, humidity, shade, and water become actionable requests.
  • Human-centered framing: the value comes from translating unclear signals into friendly, specific guidance.
  • Delight as interface design: the plant's personality makes routine care feel engaging rather than mechanical.

Learning Takeaways

  • A useful AI interface can turn weak signals into timely, understandable prompts.
  • Personality can make a tool more memorable, but the core utility is still accurate diagnosis and actionable advice.
  • The plant example generalizes to many domains where users cannot easily read system state: health, home maintenance, operations, or learning.
  • Good automation should ask for the next useful intervention rather than overwhelm the user with raw data.

Questions To Explore

  • What sensors would be needed to make plant care advice accurate enough to trust?
  • When does adding personality to an assistant improve adherence, and when does it become distracting?
  • How should an AI system express uncertainty when it infers needs from environmental data?
  • Could similar interfaces help people care for pets, homes, or medical routines?

Useful Examples/Tools/Links

  • Example requests from the clip: open the blinds, add humidity, provide shade, give more light, and water the plant.
  • Possible tool chain: soil moisture sensor, light sensor, humidity sensor, plant-care model, and conversational assistant.
  • Design pattern: translate environmental telemetry into one small, concrete user action.

Optional Post Draft

Imagine if your plant could just say: "I'm thirsty" or "too much sun."

This OpenAI short is cute, but the bigger idea is useful: AI interfaces can translate hidden signals into simple next actions.

Not raw sensor data. Not dashboards. Just: open the blinds, add humidity, move me to shade, water me.

The best AI products may feel less like complicated tools and more like clear communication from things we already care about.

notes.title: The ONLY thing AI will NEVER replace #Career #FutureOfWork #ArtificialIntelligence
notes.channel: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
notes.excerpt: This short clip argues that relationships are the durable advantage in an AI-shaped work world. Tools, platforms, channels, and tactics may change quickly, but trust and human conn
notes.html:
The ONLY thing AI will NEVER replace #Career #FutureOfWork #ArtificialIntelligence

What This Is About

This short clip argues that relationships are the durable advantage in an AI-shaped work world. Tools, platforms, channels, and tactics may change quickly, but trust and human connection compound across individuals and companies.

Important Concepts

  • Durable advantage: Relationships persist even when technical capabilities and distribution channels shift.
  • Compounding trust: The value of a relationship grows over time through repeated reliability, context, and mutual understanding.
  • Individual and company relevance: The same relationship advantage applies to careers, teams, partnerships, customers, and brands.

Learning Takeaways

  • Do not treat AI adoption as only a tooling race; relationship depth becomes more important as execution gets easier.
  • The faster tactics change, the more valuable it is to invest in people who continue to choose, trust, and remember you.
  • Companies should think of customer and partner relationships as strategic assets, not just sales or support channels.

Questions To Explore

  • Which relationships in my work compound the most, and how am I maintaining them?
  • Where am I over-investing in short-lived tactics while under-investing in durable trust?
  • How can AI help me stay more thoughtful and consistent in relationships without making them feel automated?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • Example: A consultant using AI to draft work faster still wins through client trust, judgment, and follow-through.
  • Example: A company with strong customer relationships can adapt channels and product motions more easily when the market changes.
  • Link: [Video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mWT-dBFkQdQ)

Optional Post Draft

AI may change the tools every quarter, but it does not replace the thing that keeps compounding: relationships.

The sharper takeaway: technical capability is becoming cheaper, so trust becomes more differentiated. For careers and companies, the durable work is still showing up, understanding context, and being reliable enough that people want to keep working with you.

notes.title: I Stopped Prompting AI One Task At A Time. This Works Better.
notes.channel: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
notes.excerpt: This video explains the move from one-off prompting to recurring AI workflows. Nate B. Jones calls the higher-level pattern a "loop of loops": small remembered workflows that monit
notes.html:
I Stopped Prompting AI One Task At A Time. This Works Better.

What This Is About

This video explains the move from one-off prompting to recurring AI workflows. Nate B. Jones calls the higher-level pattern a "loop of loops": small remembered workflows that monitor recurring jobs, share context with each other, stop at boundaries, and bring the human in when judgment matters.

Important Concepts

  • Prompt: A single request that produces a single response.
  • Loop: A recurring job with memory, triggers, state, quality checks, and a repeatable next action.
  • Loop of loops: A control pattern where multiple recurring workflows notice each other, exchange changed context, and coordinate handoffs.
  • Mental load: The invisible work of remembering what changed, where information lives, what is blocked, and what needs follow-up.
  • Human boundary: The point where the system pauses because a decision, message, purchase, or risk needs human judgment.

Learning Takeaways

  • The best near-term agent use cases are not magical life managers; they are practical loops around real recurring burdens.
  • Most useful work lives between apps, not inside one app. Email, calendars, school portals, CRMs, and files each hold pieces of the job, while the human currently does the wiring.
  • A good loop should remember prior state, detect changes, gather context, leave a record, and ask only when necessary.
  • A loop of loops becomes useful when several loops can coordinate: for example, a school trip loop waking packing, weather, schedule, calendar, and messaging loops.
  • Start with tedious, low-risk processes where failure is recoverable. Avoid high-stakes domains like banking as an early loop-of-loops experiment.
  • Designing agents starts with better questions: what can the system safely do, what should it ask, what record should it leave, and how should it improve next time?

Questions To Explore

  • Which recurring jobs do I still run manually even though the steps are predictable?
  • What state am I carrying in my head that could be written down, monitored, or refreshed by a loop?
  • Which apps or sources does each recurring job cross?
  • Where should each loop stop for approval?
  • Which low-risk process has enough repeated steps to justify a first loop-of-loops build?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • School trip example: school schedule, packing, weather, calendar conflict, and parent-message loops coordinate around one upcoming event.
  • Research example: separate Twitter/news/source-reading loops roll up into a daily view of what changed and what matters.
  • Sales example: call notes, objections, promised decks, pricing questions, security details, support issues, and overdue follow-ups become connected loops.
  • Household example: a grocery loop can remember when spinach was bought and suggest using it before it spoils.
  • Product-work example: use cases, Linear tickets, and PRD drafting can be connected into a lower-risk loop-of-loops experiment.
  • Link: [Video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=A4zMyjkL0Dc)

Optional Post Draft

Stop thinking of AI as "one prompt, one answer."

A more useful model is:

1. Prompt = one request.

2. Loop = one recurring job with memory.

3. Loop of loops = recurring jobs that notice each other and coordinate.

The practical question is not "How do I prompt better?" It is: "What repeated mental load am I carrying, what state does it need, and where should the system stop and ask me?"

Start with something tedious and recoverable. If it works, your week gets lighter. If it fails, you can fix the process without real damage.

notes.title: Why Anthropic Hired the Smartest Person in AI #AI #Research #News
notes.channel: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
notes.excerpt: This short analysis argues that Anthropic's hiring of John Jumper from Google DeepMind may matter more than the louder AI-news cycle around Fable. The speaker frames Jumper's move
notes.html:
Why Anthropic Hired the Smartest Person in AI #AI #Research #News

What This Is About

This short analysis argues that Anthropic's hiring of John Jumper from Google DeepMind may matter more than the louder AI-news cycle around Fable. The speaker frames Jumper's move as a talent signal: if a Nobel-winning AlphaFold researcher joins Anthropic, it may reveal where frontier AI labs believe the next valuable work will happen.

Important Concepts

  • Talent movement as strategy: senior researchers changing labs can indicate where important research agendas are forming.
  • John Jumper and AlphaFold: Jumper won a Nobel Prize for chemistry-related AI work on AlphaFold with Demis Hassabis.
  • AI for science: the clip implicitly connects frontier AI progress with scientific discovery, especially chemistry and biology.
  • Wider field of view: the speaker encourages looking past headline stories to less obvious signals.

Learning Takeaways

  • Hiring can be a stronger signal than product announcements because top researchers choose environments where they think meaningful work is possible.
  • Anthropic's position in frontier AI may be broader than model releases or consumer-facing news suggest.
  • Google DeepMind losing a high-profile AlphaFold researcher to Anthropic could be read as a competitive research signal, not just a personnel update.
  • The next phase of AI competition may depend heavily on people who can connect models to scientific discovery.

Questions To Explore

  • What role will AI-for-science researchers play inside general-purpose model labs?
  • Does Anthropic have a distinct research agenda around scientific reasoning, biology, chemistry, or model interpretability that would attract someone like Jumper?
  • How should we weigh individual talent moves against published benchmarks, product releases, and funding news?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • AlphaFold as an example of AI producing high-impact scientific capability.
  • John Jumper's move from Google DeepMind to Anthropic as a case study in reading talent flow.
  • Anthropic and Google DeepMind as labs to watch for research-driven competitive signals.

Optional Post Draft

Anthropic's biggest signal this week may not be a product headline. It may be talent.

John Jumper, one of the AlphaFold leaders and a Nobel Prize winner, moved from Google DeepMind to Anthropic. That matters because researchers at this level usually optimize for where they think important work can actually happen.

Worth watching: whether the next frontier is not just better chatbots, but AI systems that help with scientific discovery.

notes.html:
员工蒸馏：水没有了，鱼要变成“四维生物”

What This Is About

这段讨论把 Meta 引发争议的“员工蒸馏”放进更大的 AI 就业转型中：公司希望把员工经验沉淀进模型，但员工若预期自己会被替代，就可能减少知识分享甚至污染训练数据。长期来看，大厂岗位可能持续收缩，个人需要从“在公司之间跳槽”转向建立更独立的价值创造方式。

Important Concepts

  • 员工蒸馏：把员工的流程、判断与隐性知识转成公司可复用的 AI 能力。
  • 激励错位：公司追求知识资产化，员工则担心知识被提取后失去岗位，双方因此产生对立。
  • 知识投毒风险：强制采集可能促使员工隐藏关键经验、提供低质量样本或故意加入错误信息。
  • “水坑干涸”比喻：在大厂之间跳槽只是鱼换水坑；当岗位总量下降时，真正的适应是获得离开水也能生存的能力。

Learning Takeaways

  • AI 转型不仅是技术部署问题，也是组织激励与信任问题；没有合理的利益安排，最宝贵的隐性知识反而最难被捕获。
  • 企业减少用工可能是渐进但快速的结构性变化，未来三到五年内更多人会被迫重新定义职业身份。
  • 与其只优化下一次求职，更值得培养可独立交付、创业协作和直接服务市场的能力。

Questions To Explore

  • 企业如何让员工分享知识，同时避免“教会模型后被替代”的恐惧？
  • 哪些专业能力可以被流程化蒸馏，哪些依赖关系、判断和责任承担而难以复制？
  • 个人应优先建设哪些资产，才能从雇员转向创业者或小团队联合创始人？

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • Meta“员工蒸馏”争议可作为研究 AI 知识管理与劳动关系的案例。
  • 《三体》中“把水弄干”的比喻适合解释岗位池整体收缩，而非单一公司的裁员。
  • 可建立个人能力清单：可自动化任务、不可替代判断、客户关系、分发渠道与可复用作品。

Optional Post Draft

如果大厂岗位是水坑，跳槽只是鱼从一个水坑跳到另一个水坑。AI 真正带来的挑战，是水本身在减少。下一步不只是学会用 AI 提效，而是把能力变成可以独立交付、直接创造价值的产品或服务——让鱼长出翅膀。

notes.title: Meet the ChatGPT Futures, Class of 2026
notes.excerpt: This OpenAI video introduces the ChatGPT Futures Class of 2026 through a fast montage of young builders using AI across science, accessibility, education, aviation, national securi
notes.html:
Meet the ChatGPT Futures, Class of 2026

What This Is About

This OpenAI video introduces the ChatGPT Futures Class of 2026 through a fast montage of young builders using AI across science, accessibility, education, aviation, national security, robotics, medicine, dating, scholarships, and disaster response. The central message is that AI lowers the cost of turning ambitious ideas into working projects.

Important Concepts

  • AI as a creation accelerator: builders use ChatGPT to move from idea to working project faster.
  • Cross-domain use cases: examples span astronomy, accessibility, education, aviation, national security, robotics, medicine, consumer products, scholarships, and emergency response.
  • Student and early-career entrepreneurship: the video emphasizes young people building serious projects now.
  • Opportunity expansion: AI can make high-skill workflows more approachable to people without traditional starting advantages.

Learning Takeaways

  • AI's impact is not limited to software; it can support research, learning, robotics, search, planning, and public-interest applications.
  • The strongest examples connect AI to a specific bottleneck: searching galaxy images, designing accessible education, finding scholarships, or improving rescue work.
  • AI tools are most compelling when they help people act on an existing mission, not when they are treated as the whole project.
  • The video frames 2026 as a moment when creative ambition has unusually high leverage.

Questions To Explore

  • What makes a youth AI project robust enough to move beyond demo stage?
  • Which domains benefit most from AI lowering the cost of prototyping?
  • How should programs like ChatGPT Futures measure impact: shipped products, learning outcomes, research progress, or community benefit?
  • What support do young builders need after the first prototype works?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • A search engine for 100 million galaxy images.
  • An educational game platform for blind and visually impaired students.
  • Using ChatGPT to go from zero to a fully licensed pilot.
  • AI for national security.
  • A robot being sent to the International Space Station.
  • AI-supported drug discovery, dating apps, scholarship discovery, and disaster survivor search.

Optional Post Draft

The most interesting part of ChatGPT Futures is the range of projects.

Students are using AI for galaxy search, accessible education, aviation, robotics, medicine, scholarships, and disaster response. The pattern is not “AI replaces the project.” It is “AI helps someone move faster on a real problem.”

Good reminder: the next wave of AI builders may come from people who know a problem deeply, then use AI to remove the first few barriers to building.

notes.excerpt: Zendesk CEO Tom Eggemeier describes how he turns AI ideas into action: stay focused on a clear problem or opportunity, put the right team on it, stay close to customers, and measur
notes.html:
How Zendesk CEO Tom Eggemeier goes from Idea to Action

What This Is About

Zendesk CEO Tom Eggemeier describes how he turns AI ideas into action: stay focused on a clear problem or opportunity, put the right team on it, stay close to customers, and measure whether AI is improving automated resolutions and customer satisfaction.

Important Concepts

  • Speed and quality: AI transformation requires moving quickly without losing the customer outcome.
  • Focus: Going too broad prevents meaningful workflow change.
  • Team quality: The right team is central to turning AI strategy into actual work.
  • Customer proximity: Eggemeier calls three customers each Monday from weekly NPS survey responses.
  • AI-first operating metrics: Zendesk tracks automated resolutions and customer satisfaction as practical signals of AI impact.

Learning Takeaways

  • Enterprise AI work should begin with a specific customer problem or opportunity, not a vague mandate to "use AI."
  • Leaders need operating rituals that keep AI transformation connected to customer reality.
  • NPS follow-up calls can surface qualitative context behind metrics and keep executives close to real users.
  • AI-first transformation should include process change, employee upskilling, and software engineering speed, not only model adoption.
  • Boards and executive teams should ask whether they are moving fast enough with tools like Codex and with broader engineering transformation.

Questions To Explore

  • What customer workflow would change if we focused AI efforts more narrowly?
  • Which two or three metrics best show whether AI is actually improving service quality?
  • Do leaders have a regular customer-listening ritual, or are they relying only on dashboards?
  • Where does the organization need upskilling before AI can change the nature of work?
  • How should the board evaluate AI transformation speed without encouraging shallow adoption?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • Example: Use weekly NPS responses to choose a few customers for direct executive follow-up.
  • Example: Track automated resolutions alongside customer satisfaction so efficiency does not hide quality regressions.
  • Tool: Codex as part of faster software engineering and AI-first workflow transformation.
  • Link: [Video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=27-GjDIWGo0)

Optional Post Draft

Zendesk CEO Tom Eggemeier's AI operating advice is simple but useful:

Focus on a real problem. Put the right team on it. Stay close to customers. Measure both automation and satisfaction.

The interesting part is the Monday ritual: he calls three customers from NPS responses. That keeps AI transformation grounded in what customers actually experience, not just what dashboards say.
