notes.excerpt: This short teaser argues that the important strategic story from Apple's June 8 WWDC is not simply whether Siri is catching up in AI. It suggests Apple's announcements may have bro
notes.html:
Siri isn't the real headline at WWDC #apple #ai #wwdc (Full Video Thursday)

What This Is About

This short teaser argues that the important strategic story from Apple's June 8 WWDC is not simply whether Siri is catching up in AI. It suggests Apple's announcements may have broader implications for AI hardware and Nvidia, potentially connecting to Jensen Huang's previously announced laptop-chip strategy.

Important Concepts

  • Headline versus underlying strategy: Siri improvements may distract from more consequential platform and hardware shifts.
  • Software-hardware linkage: Announcements at a software conference can still reshape chip and device competition.
  • Competitive positioning: Apple's broader AI strategy may matter directly to Nvidia and Jensen Huang.
  • AI value capture: The teaser frames the story around which companies and leaders capture the largest economic upside from AI.

Learning Takeaways

  • AI strategy should be evaluated across the full stack, including software, devices, chips, and distribution.
  • The most discussed product announcement is not always the most strategically important one.
  • Apple's platform moves may affect companies outside its obvious software competitors.
  • The transcript is a preview, so its central thesis remains speculative until supported by the promised full analysis.

Questions To Explore

  • Which WWDC announcements specifically affect Nvidia's laptop-chip or broader AI hardware strategy?
  • How does Apple's device ecosystem change the economics of on-device AI?
  • Is Siri strategically important on its own, or mainly as an interface into Apple's wider AI platform?
  • What evidence supports the claim that WWDC changes the race for AI value capture?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • Apple's WWDC announcements
  • Siri AI
  • Nvidia and Jensen Huang
  • Nvidia's announced laptop chip
  • Full follow-up analysis promised for Thursday

Optional Post Draft

The loudest WWDC debate is whether Siri finally caught up. But the more useful question may be what Apple's software announcements imply for AI hardware, devices, and Nvidia. In AI strategy, the headline feature can be less important than the platform shift underneath it.

notes.title: Codex for Finance: Faster Reports, Dashboards, and Decisions
notes.excerpt: OpenAI's finance technology lead describes Codex as a general workflow tool for finance, procurement, and accounting rather than only a coding assistant. The central use case is co
notes.html:
Codex for Finance: Faster Reports, Dashboards, and Decisions

What This Is About

OpenAI's finance technology lead describes Codex as a general workflow tool for finance, procurement, and accounting rather than only a coding assistant. The central use case is converting messy, manual finance work into repeatable workflows.

Important Concepts

  • Reusable skills: Build capabilities once so other Codex workflows can apply them consistently.
  • Internal finance apps: Create purpose-built tools across finance, procurement, and accounting.
  • Workflow standardization: Turn recurring, loosely defined processes into repeatable workflows.
  • Broad knowledge-work support: Use Codex for outputs such as dashboards, slides, memos, and review materials.

Learning Takeaways

  • Codex can support both high-complexity internal applications and simple everyday deliverables.
  • Finance automation opportunities extend beyond reporting into month-end work, vendor risk reviews, and journal-entry preparation support.
  • The largest benefit comes from making recurring processes consistent and reusable, not merely completing one-off tasks faster.

Questions To Explore

  • Which finance workflows are repeatable enough to encode as reusable Codex skills?
  • What review and approval controls are required for journal-entry preparation and vendor risk analysis?
  • How should teams measure time saved, accuracy, and adoption across these workflows?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • Month-end workflow support
  • Executive slides and memos
  • Custom finance dashboards
  • Vendor risk reviews
  • Journal-entry preparation support
  • Internal apps for finance, procurement, and accounting

Optional Post Draft

Codex in finance is not mainly about writing code. The bigger opportunity is turning messy recurring work into repeatable workflows: month-end, dashboards, executive slides, vendor reviews, and even journal-entry preparation. The best starting point may be the workflow your team repeats every month but still rebuilds by hand.

notes.title: Codex for data science
notes.excerpt: This demo presents Codex with the data analytics plugin as an agentic analyst that gathers context from multiple systems, builds business-impact reports, supports live edits, expos
notes.html:
Codex for data science

What This Is About

This demo presents Codex with the data analytics plugin as an agentic analyst that gathers context from multiple systems, builds business-impact reports, supports live edits, exposes its data sources and workflow, and exports results into leadership-ready templates.

Important Concepts

  • Data analytics plugin: Packages skills, data sources, workflows, and tools around a team's specific business problems.
  • Agentic analysis: Codex gathers relevant context and produces reports, charts, and analysis with limited manual coordination.
  • Editable artifacts: Reports remain interactive so users can refine charts and presentation choices during analysis.
  • Workflow transparency: Users can inspect the data sources and process behind generated reports.
  • Template-aware export: Finished analysis can be exported into established formats such as Google Slides.

Learning Takeaways

  • A useful analytics agent must connect to business context and data sources, not only generate charts.
  • Interactive refinement is important because initial analytical output often needs to be made clearer for business users.
  • Transparency into sources and workflow makes generated analysis easier to validate and operationalize.
  • The workflow can span research, analysis, editing, and leadership-ready delivery in one place.

Questions To Explore

  • How are metric definitions and data quality checks enforced across connected systems?
  • What review steps are needed before an agent-generated report reaches leadership?
  • Which parts of recurring reports can be standardized as reusable skills and templates?
  • How should teams evaluate whether the generated analysis identifies real business impact?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • Codex data analytics plugin
  • Multi-source business-impact reports
  • Editable charts and analytical artifacts
  • Data-source and workflow inspection
  • Google Slides export using company templates

Optional Post Draft

An AI analyst becomes much more useful when it can do the whole reporting loop: gather context from multiple systems, build the analysis, let you refine the charts, show its sources, and export into the exact template leadership expects. The key is not just faster charts; it is keeping analysis, validation, editing, and delivery in one workflow.

notes.title: Operationalizing AI in workflows: Lee Spacagna, Solutions Engineer, OpenAI
notes.excerpt: Lee Spacagna demonstrates how workspace agents can fill the automation gap between individual AI use and large, centrally built AI systems. A business user builds and deploys a chi
notes.html:
Operationalizing AI in workflows: Lee Spacagna, Solutions Engineer, OpenAI

What This Is About

Lee Spacagna demonstrates how workspace agents can fill the automation gap between individual AI use and large, centrally built AI systems. A business user builds and deploys a chief-of-staff agent that connects to everyday tools, prepares daily and meeting briefs, and posts results where the team already works.

Important Concepts

  • Middle-layer automation: Team and department workflows sit between personal AI tools and major enterprise AI systems.
  • Agents versus chat: Agents are delegated meaningful tasks and can use tools, gather context, and take actions.
  • Applications and connectors: Email, calendar, Teams, SharePoint, Salesforce, and custom systems supply context and action surfaces.
  • Skills: Reusable instructions capture tribal knowledge, conventions, output formats, and workflow steps.
  • Governance at scale: A management layer is needed when organizations deploy thousands of agents.

Learning Takeaways

  • Start with a role-specific workflow whose context is scattered across meetings, documents, emails, and systems.
  • Natural-language agent building can let domain experts create useful first versions without code or prompt-engineering expertise.
  • Agents are most useful when their output appears inside the existing workflow, such as a Teams channel or prepared email draft.
  • Scaling agents requires permissions, evaluation, shared context, and centralized governance.

Questions To Explore

  • Which team workflows are repeatable enough to delegate but contextual enough to benefit from an agent?
  • What actions should require explicit human approval?
  • How should skills be versioned, evaluated, and owned as business processes change?
  • What governance model prevents duplicated, conflicting, or over-permissioned agents?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • Chief-of-staff agent that creates a 9:00 a.m. daily brief from email, calendar, and team applications.
  • Meeting-prep skill using Salesforce and SharePoint context.
  • Posting briefs directly into Microsoft Teams after permission approval.
  • Potential financial-services workflows: KYC onboarding, AML investigations, and relationship management.
  • Frontier, described as a platform for deploying and managing agents at scale.

Optional Post Draft

The missing layer in enterprise AI is often team-level automation. Personal copilots help individuals; major AI systems transform products. Workspace agents connect the two by letting domain experts package tools, context, and tribal knowledge into role-specific coworkers, while governance and approvals keep their actions controlled.

notes.title: OpenAI on OpenAI: Stacie Faggioli, Business Finance Officer Applications, OpenAI
notes.excerpt: Stacie Faggioli explains how OpenAI's finance organization is redesigning workflows around AI rather than attaching AI to old processes. She shares examples ranging from individual
notes.html:
OpenAI on OpenAI: Stacie Faggioli, Business Finance Officer Applications, OpenAI

What This Is About

Stacie Faggioli explains how OpenAI's finance organization is redesigning workflows around AI rather than attaching AI to old processes. She shares examples ranging from individual analysis tools to agents embedded in procurement, credit checks, contract review, and vendor risk.

Important Concepts

  • AI-native by design: Reimagine the workflow, spread best practices, and reconsider team structure around agents.
  • Headcount leverage: OpenAI says its finance team is about 20% the size of comparable technology peers.
  • Embedded engineering: Engineers sit alongside finance subject-matter experts to speed deployment and iteration.
  • Auditable AI analysis: Finance outputs still need traceable formulas, sources, QA, and evaluations.
  • Workflow agents: Agents orchestrate systems and handle repeatable work without requiring team size to grow linearly.

Learning Takeaways

  • Deploy early and iterate quickly; waiting for a "final" AI capability prevents teams from building practical experience.
  • Put tools in the hands of people closest to the data and workflow because they identify the highest-value use cases.
  • AI creates more leverage when it changes the operating process, not merely when it accelerates one manual step.
  • Human review remains essential for consequential financial outputs, even when agents prepare most of the work.

Questions To Explore

  • Which finance processes have enough volume and standardization to justify an agent?
  • What evaluations and approval gates are needed before AI-generated finance outputs reach executives or customers?
  • How should organizations measure headcount leverage without degrading controls or employee development?
  • Which roles should embed engineers directly with domain experts?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • Investor-relations agent producing consistent, data-grounded diligence responses.
  • ChatGPT for Excel building a traceable LBO model from an equity research PDF.
  • Codex dashboards for marketing ROI and sales-product adoption signals.
  • Reusable Codex skills automating monthly compute-margin reporting.
  • Procurement, credit-check, contract-review, and vendor-risk agents embedded in operational systems.

Optional Post Draft

AI-native finance is not "use a chatbot on the existing spreadsheet." It means redesigning the workflow, embedding engineers with finance experts, and giving agents repeatable operational work. The strongest lesson: democratize the tools, but keep traceability, QA, and human review for consequential decisions.

notes.html:
OpenAI Investor Innovation Day

What This Is About

A short set of enterprise customer perspectives on moving from asking AI questions to using it to do operational work. The examples emphasize connecting company context, accelerating analysis, and spreading reusable AI workflows across an organization.

Important Concepts

  • From asking to doing: The larger productivity shift comes when AI performs work and helps solve difficult business problems, rather than only answering questions.
  • Context-connected assistance: Connecting a deal folder or other business context enables useful real-time questions about active work.
  • Organizational flywheel: Broad access and hundreds of shared GPTs can compound into reusable workflows across day-to-day operations.
  • Force multiplication: AI tools can reduce startup time for analysis and increase what employees can accomplish.

Learning Takeaways

  • Enterprise transformation requires pairing AI access with people who can identify and solve hard, high-value problems.
  • Tools become more useful when they can work with relevant organizational context instead of isolated prompts.
  • Adoption scales when employees create workflows that others can reuse, turning individual gains into organization-wide improvements.
  • Concrete tools such as the ChatGPT Excel plugin can meaningfully speed up the first steps of company analysis.

Questions To Explore

  • What governance and quality checks are needed when hundreds of employee-built GPTs enter daily operations?
  • Which workflows benefit most from connected deal-folder context, and which should remain manually reviewed?
  • How should an organization measure whether broad AI licensing produces durable business value?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • ChatGPT Enterprise licenses for broad employee access
  • Custom GPTs embedded in day-to-day operations
  • ChatGPT Excel plugin for accelerating company analysis
  • Deal-folder connections for real-time, context-aware questions

Optional Post Draft

Enterprise AI adoption changes when teams move from asking questions to getting work done. The strongest examples connect AI to real business context, speed up recurring analysis, and let employees share reusable workflows. The opportunity is not just individual productivity; it is building an organizational flywheel around better ways of working.

notes.id: 2026-06-05-再访田渊栋-46-5亿美金估值的rsi-与ai自进化-neolabs特辑-101视频播客.md
notes.file: 2026-06-05-再访田渊栋-46-5亿美金估值的rsi-与ai自进化-neolabs特辑-101视频播客.md
notes.title: 再访田渊栋：46.5亿美金估值的RSI，与AI自进化｜Neolabs特辑【101视频播客】
notes.excerpt: 这期访谈围绕田渊栋加入 Recursive Superintelligence（RSI）后的研究方向展开。RSI 希望通过递归自我改进，让 AI 自动化部分研究过程、发现新知识，并最终提高知识发现速率。访谈也讨论了预训练与强化学习、潜在空间推理、AI 科研商业化、NeoLab 的组织优势，以及 AI 对工程岗位和前沿实验室竞争的影响。 田渊栋加入估值 46.
notes.html:
再访田渊栋：46.5亿美金估值的RSI，与AI自进化｜Neolabs特辑【101视频播客】

What This Is About

这期访谈围绕田渊栋加入 Recursive Superintelligence（RSI）后的研究方向展开。RSI 希望通过递归自我改进，让 AI 自动化部分研究过程、发现新知识，并最终提高知识发现速率。访谈也讨论了预训练与强化学习、潜在空间推理、AI 科研商业化、NeoLab 的组织优势，以及 AI 对工程岗位和前沿实验室竞争的影响。

Important Concepts

  • 递归自我改进：用 AI 优化 AI 研究与训练过程，让系统逐步产生更强能力和新知识。
  • 知识发现速率：RSI 的目标是把计算资源转化为新的知识、洞察和研究结果。
  • 结果导向评估：当任务越来越主观，评估可能从中间 benchmark 转向最终产出是否有用。
  • 可解释性：既是安全机制，也能提前判断训练方向、减少昂贵实验的浪费。
  • 潜在空间推理：非语言 latent token 可以同时承载多个思路，理论上比逐字语言推理更高效。
  • 预训练决定强化学习上限：RL 可以放大已有的正确“种子”，但难以创造预训练中完全不存在的能力。
  • 小团队优势：AI 发展速度超过大型组织的沟通和重组速度，小型高密度团队更容易快速对齐。

Learning Takeaways

  • AI 自动化科研不是一次性的 0 到 1 突破，而是一条很长的能力阶梯；每一步都可能产生可商业化价值。
  • RSI 选择研究自我改进范式，是因为垂直产品容易被更通用的模型覆盖，而持续提升 AI 的方法更具长期性。
  • AI coding agent 的成熟改变了创业时机：小团队可以用更少的人完成过去需要大组织才能完成的工作。
  • 强化学习的效果依赖预训练形成的表示与推理模式；没有正确候选时，后训练无法凭空放大能力。
  • 下一波高 ROI 自动化可能是 AI research，因为顶级研究人才昂贵，研究成果的价值可能远高于代码生成。
  • AI 工具增强后，人才价值更偏向经验、研究品味、方向判断和亲手完成事情的能力。
  • 前沿模型竞争不仅是技术竞争，也越来越是组织架构、执行速度、产品体验和成本结构的竞争。

Questions To Explore

  • 如何验证自动化科研系统发现的是可泛化能力，而不是对评估指标的过拟合？
  • 当 AI 的研究能力超过人类时，评估者和可解释性机制应如何升级？
  • 潜在空间推理如何在保持效率的同时提供足够的可审查性？
  • 合成数据、持续学习和新训练范式中，哪条路线最可能突破预训练成本瓶颈？
  • 小型 NeoLab 如何在算力有限的情况下建立可持续的研究和商业护城河？
  • AI research 的第一批高价值产品会来自模型训练、材料、药物，还是通用研究助手？

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • RSI（Recursive Superintelligence）：以递归自我改进和自动化研究为核心的新实验室。
  • Coconut：田渊栋参与的连续思维链与潜在空间推理研究。
  • The AI Scientist-v2：访谈提到的自动化科学研究工作。
  • AlphaEvolve：用大模型持续变异和组合算法的 AI for Science 示例。
  • AppSec/coding agents 带来的小团队生产力变化，可作为组织设计研究案例。
  • Video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=F7oNfszczVc

Optional Post Draft

田渊栋加入估值 46.5 亿美元的 RSI 后，押注的不是一个具体 AI 产品，而是一个更底层的范式：让 AI 帮助 AI 自己进化。

几个值得记住的判断：

1. 强化学习可以把已有的正确答案从第 200 名提到第 1 名，但如果预训练里根本没有正确“种子”，RL 也无能为力。

2. AI 自动化科研不是突然跨越的 0 到 1，而是一条很长的阶梯，每爬一步都可能有商业价值。

3. AI coding agent 已经让小团队获得过去大组织才有的生产力；前沿模型竞争越来越像组织效率竞争。

4. Coding 之后，下一个高 ROI 方向可能是 AI research，因为新药、新材料或新训练方法的价值可能是天文数字。

真正的长期问题不是 AI 能不能做研究，而是人类如何评估、理解并安全使用它发现的新知识。

notes.id: 2026-06-05-从-健身芭比-到-女企业家-帕梅拉有多会-赚.md
notes.file: 2026-06-05-从-健身芭比-到-女企业家-帕梅拉有多会-赚.md
notes.title: 从\"健身芭比\"到\"女企业家\"，帕梅拉有多会\"赚\"？
notes.excerpt: This is a 小Lin interview with Pamela Reif about how she grew from a fitness creator into a global business operator. The conversation covers her accidental product strategy, why si
notes.html:
从"健身芭比"到"女企业家"，帕梅拉有多会"赚"？

What This Is About

This is a 小Lin interview with Pamela Reif about how she grew from a fitness creator into a global business operator. The conversation covers her accidental product strategy, why silent workout videos became globally reusable, how China became her largest follower market, and how she thinks about creator relevance, brand building, health, and discipline.

Important Concepts

  • Silent, repeatable content: Pamela's no-talking workout format started as a gut-feel usability choice, then became her signature because people could replay videos without hearing the same script.
  • Global content can travel before the creator knows it: her China audience first grew through reposted videos before she officially took over platform accounts.
  • Market-specific fitness preferences: China skews more toward beginner, slim-leg, and lean-body content, while Europe and South America show stronger demand for muscle definition and heavier training.
  • Creator-to-business funnel: free workout videos create reach, the app deepens engagement, and products/brand deals monetize the relationship.
  • Useful content compounds: Pamela argues longevity comes from creating things people actually use in daily life, not just being an interesting personality.
  • Healthy lifestyle basics: sleep, nutrition, gut health, movement, strength, and nervous system regulation matter more than chasing a single body image.

Learning Takeaways

  • A strong format can come from a small friction insight. Removing talking made the videos easier to repeat and easier to localize without translation.
  • China is not just another distribution market for her; it is her biggest follower market, with different platform behavior, consumer habits, and product expectations.
  • Product category choice should fit operating cadence. Pamela avoided clothing partly because seasonal collections require constant reinvention, while food products can be optimized and sold longer.
  • Building a China-specific brand likely requires more than importing European products; snacks, protein bars, and health positioning need local strategy.
  • Social media success can shift from inspiration to attention pressure. Creators need to help audiences use content rather than only compare themselves to it.
  • Relevance is easier to sustain when the audience has a practical reason to return: workouts, recipes, app plans, snacks, hair products, or routines.

Questions To Explore

  • What kind of health or fitness product would best match Pamela's China audience: food, supplements, app plans, community programs, or something else?
  • How should global creators adapt naming and positioning without fragmenting their brand identity?
  • Can "usefulness" be measured as a creator KPI, separate from engagement or follower count?
  • What operational systems help a creator keep content quality high while shifting more time into business building?
  • How much does local platform ownership matter when an audience initially grows through unofficial reposting?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • Pamela's no-talking workout videos as an example of replay-friendly content design.
  • Beginner standing legs and abs video: weak response in Europe but huge response in China, illustrating market-specific demand.
  • Naturally Pam: food-product brand with roughly 15 million annual revenue mentioned in the interview.
  • Pam app: workout plans and healthy recipes, cited as having 6 million downloads.
  • China platform account takeover: passport verification and reclaiming accounts after fake profiles began selling products on her behalf.

Optional Post Draft

帕梅拉这期最有意思的不是「健身博主怎么火」，而是一个创作者怎么变成企业家。

几个点很值得记：

1. 她的健身视频不说话，一开始不是宏大战略，只是觉得用户重复练同一个视频时不想反复听同一句话。结果这个小设计变成了全球化优势。

2. 中国是她最大的粉丝市场，但中国用户喜欢的训练内容、身材审美、零食习惯都和欧洲不一样。全球品牌不能只靠搬运。

3. 她没有先定一个十年大目标，而是每天有想法、纪律性执行，然后机会自己浮出来。

4. 她说自己能长期相关，是因为她的内容和产品对用户「有用」：训练、食谱、App、食品、洗护。

我最喜欢的一句潜台词：创作者真正的护城河，不只是被喜欢，而是被用到。

notes.title: Introducing Sites in Codex
notes.excerpt: Sites in Codex lets teams turn trusted workspace context into secure, shareable applications without separately setting up hosting, authentication, storage, databases, or deploymen
notes.html:
Introducing Sites in Codex

What This Is About

Sites in Codex lets teams turn trusted workspace context into secure, shareable applications without separately setting up hosting, authentication, storage, databases, or deployment code. Teams can build and refine focused internal tools conversationally, then share them with specific users or the whole workspace.

Important Concepts

  • Managed application infrastructure: hosting, authentication, storage, and database support are included.
  • Context-powered app building: Codex plugins and skills can bring in analysis and material from tools a team already uses.
  • Conversational iteration: users can keep refining a published application through prompts instead of deployment workflows.
  • Workspace asset model: sites can be shared and managed like other workspace resources, with viewers signing in through ChatGPT.
  • Focused internal apps: the examples emphasize decision support and coordination rather than broad, complex software products.

Learning Takeaways

  • Sites lowers the cost of turning information trapped in documents and slides into interactive tools people can actually use.
  • The strongest use cases combine trusted source material, a narrow workflow, and a clear audience.
  • Built-in infrastructure makes lightweight internal applications accessible to non-specialist builders.
  • Sharing and authentication are core product features, not deployment tasks left for the builder.

Questions To Explore

  • What governance and approval controls exist for publishing sites built from sensitive workspace data?
  • How are source updates reflected in an already published site?
  • What database and integration limits apply to more complex internal workflows?
  • How should teams decide whether an idea belongs in a site, a document, or a full software project?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • Account Lens: a pre-meeting app combining customer context, approved talking points, and financial metrics.
  • Event preparation hub: agenda, attendees, approved messages, conversation starters, and a before-you-go plan.
  • Compass Memo: an executive investment memo covering proposal, budget, guardrails, risks, reviewers, and evidence.
  • Other suggested uses: forecasting dashboards, onboarding hubs, product council briefs, and interactive data stories.
  • Video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VRvC5smyzso

Optional Post Draft

Codex Sites turns a useful idea into a secure internal app without making the team manage hosting, authentication, storage, databases, or deployment code.

The interesting shift is not just faster app building. It is turning trusted context that would normally live in documents or slides into focused tools people can open and use: an account review dashboard, event briefing hub, or investment decision memo.

The best starting point seems simple: pick one narrow team workflow, connect the trusted material, and make the next decision easier.

notes.title: 1Password One Shots with Codex
notes.excerpt: 1Password describes how it uses OpenAI models and Codex to shorten the path from planning to production. The company applies AI across engineering and knowledge-work teams, embeds
notes.html:
1Password One Shots with Codex

What This Is About

1Password describes how it uses OpenAI models and Codex to shorten the path from planning to production. The company applies AI across engineering and knowledge-work teams, embeds its security policies into reusable AppSec skills, and is building an internal AI SRE agent.

Important Concepts

  • One-shot delivery: moving from an idea to a working production feature in a much shorter cycle.
  • Reusable AppSec skills: encoding company security policy and security posture into instructions that AI-assisted engineering workflows can apply.
  • AI SRE agent: an internal reliability engineering agent built with models and APIs.
  • Broad interaction modes: chat works naturally for finance and marketing teams, while Codex supports engineering delivery.
  • Short feedback loops: faster UI changes allow customer feedback to reach production sooner.

Learning Takeaways

  • The largest engineering gain is often cycle-time reduction, not simply faster code generation.
  • Company-specific skills can make security requirements part of the default development workflow.
  • AI adoption should match the user: chat interfaces for knowledge workers and agentic coding tools for engineers.
  • Faster feedback-to-production loops can improve customer satisfaction and business outcomes.
  • Security companies can use AI effectively when their policies are encoded into the workflow rather than treated as a final review.

Questions To Explore

  • How does 1Password validate that AppSec skills consistently enforce its security posture?
  • Which production changes are appropriate for one-shot generation, and which still require deeper review?
  • What permissions and safeguards will the internal AI SRE agent have?
  • How does the company measure gains in launch speed, quality, and customer satisfaction?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • AppSec skills built from 1Password's security policies and posture.
  • Internal AI SRE agent built using OpenAI models and APIs.
  • Codex for accelerating feature launches and customer-driven UI changes.
  • Chat interfaces for finance, marketing, and other knowledge-worker teams.
  • Video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xZ5J81Lwyk8

Optional Post Draft

1Password's most useful AI lesson is not "generate more code." It is shortening the full loop from planning to a production feature.

Three practical patterns stand out:

1. Encode company security policy into reusable AppSec skills.

2. Give each team the right interface: chat for knowledge work, Codex for engineering.

3. Use faster delivery to turn customer feedback into product changes sooner.

AI becomes more valuable when company standards are built into the workflow, not added after generation.

notes.id: 2026-06-04-谷歌三次失败的-星链梦-与spacex的9亿投资.md
notes.file: 2026-06-04-谷歌三次失败的-星链梦-与spacex的9亿投资.md
notes.title: 谷歌三次失败的“星链梦”与SpaceX的9亿投资
notes.excerpt: 这期短视频讲的是谷歌为什么会在 2015 年与富达一起向 SpaceX 投资 10 亿美元。核心线索是：谷歌为了扩大互联网用户规模，曾三次尝试做“让偏远地区联网”的基础设施项目，但气球、太阳能无人机和低轨卫星团队都没能跑通，最终转向投资已经在推进 Starlink 思路的 SpaceX。 谷歌为什么会投 SpaceX？
notes.html:
谷歌三次失败的“星链梦”与SpaceX的9亿投资

What This Is About

这期短视频讲的是谷歌为什么会在 2015 年与富达一起向 SpaceX 投资 10 亿美元。核心线索是：谷歌为了扩大互联网用户规模，曾三次尝试做“让偏远地区联网”的基础设施项目，但气球、太阳能无人机和低轨卫星团队都没能跑通，最终转向投资已经在推进 Starlink 思路的 SpaceX。

Important Concepts

  • 互联网增长与基础设施：谷歌需要让偏远地区和发展中国家的更多人联网，才能继续扩大搜索、广告和互联网服务市场。
  • Project Loon：用平流层气球提供 4G 覆盖，用户无需特殊设备，但气球寿命、漂移控制和阵列维护成本很高。
  • Titan Aerospace：太阳能无人机方案设想在约 2 万米高空连续飞行多年并发射 4G 信号，但材料、电池和原型机可靠性不足。
  • Google Satellites：谷歌曾计划发射 360 颗低轨卫星，并挖来卫星互联网先驱 Greg Wyler，但团队很快离开并创办 OneWeb。
  • 战略投资替代自研：当内部项目连续受挫时，投资 SpaceX 变成了押注卫星互联网的更现实路径。

Learning Takeaways

  • 大平台公司的增长瓶颈有时不是产品功能，而是底层接入能力；没有网络覆盖，就没有新增互联网市场。
  • “疯狂项目”即使技术想象力强，也必须被单位经济、维护复杂度和可靠性约束检验。
  • 谷歌的 SpaceX 投资可以看作一次从自建基础设施到押注外部基础设施公司的战略切换。
  • Greg Wyler 的流动说明早期卫星互联网赛道高度依赖少数关键人才和团队经验。
  • 如果 SpaceX 按视频中提到的高估值上市，谷歌这笔投资可能成为其历史上回报最突出的战略投资之一。

Questions To Explore

  • 谷歌当年投资 SpaceX 时是否获得了 Starlink、数据传输或云业务方面的特殊合作权利？
  • Project Loon、Titan Aerospace 和低轨卫星方案分别失败在技术、成本还是组织优先级上？
  • OneWeb、Starlink 和后来 Amazon Kuiper 的技术路线、频谱资源和商业模式有什么关键差异？
  • 如果卫星互联网普及，搜索、广告、地图、云服务和移动操作系统会受益最多的是哪一层？

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • Video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=y8aLyakgGbQ
  • Companies/projects mentioned: Project Loon, Titan Aerospace, Google Satellites, OneWeb, SpaceX, Starlink, Fidelity.

Optional Post Draft

谷歌为什么会投 SpaceX？

一个有趣角度：这不是单纯的财务投资，而是谷歌三次“星链梦”失败后的战略转向。

它先试过平流层气球，想给偏远地区发 4G；又买过太阳能无人机公司，设想让飞机在高空连续飞 5 年；还组建过低轨卫星团队。但这些项目都卡在寿命、成本、材料、电池、团队流失等问题上。

所以 2015 年谷歌和富达向 SpaceX 投了 10 亿美元。与其继续自己造一套全球联网基础设施，不如押注那个最可能把低轨卫星网络做出来的公司。

notes.title: What Codex Unlocks for Zapier
notes.excerpt: This OpenAI customer clip explains how Zapier uses Codex to turn scattered remote-company context into detailed Jira planning artifacts. The core example is generating full-scope t
notes.html:
What Codex Unlocks for Zapier

What This Is About

This OpenAI customer clip explains how Zapier uses Codex to turn scattered remote-company context into detailed Jira planning artifacts. The core example is generating full-scope tickets and epics from multiple knowledge sources much faster than a manual research process.

Important Concepts

  • Full-scope Jira ticket generation: Codex helps turn research and context into detailed implementation instructions.
  • MCP and SDK context access: Zapier uses Zapier MCP and SDK to bring external context into Codex.
  • Hours instead of weeks: the stated productivity gain is compressing research-heavy ticket preparation from weeks to hours.
  • Customer unblocking: faster planning artifacts help teams act on customer needs sooner.

Learning Takeaways

  • Agent value increases when it can reach the systems where company knowledge actually lives.
  • Ticket writing is a strong agent use case because it requires synthesis, specificity, and cross-source context rather than pure code generation.
  • The useful output is not just a summary; it is an epic with detailed enough instructions to move engineering work forward.
  • For automation platforms like Zapier, Codex can become part of the workflow design layer, not only the implementation layer.

Questions To Explore

  • What makes a Codex-generated Jira ticket "ready" for engineering pickup?
  • Which MCP-connected sources are most important for reducing planning time?
  • How should teams compare generated tickets against manually written tickets for quality?
  • What review step prevents old or contradictory source context from becoming bad implementation guidance?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • Jira epic and ticket generation.
  • Zapier MCP and SDK for pulling context into Codex.
  • Slack, Google Docs, and Coda as upstream knowledge systems mentioned in the related Zapier clip.
  • Incident response and postmortems as adjacent artifacts Codex can help produce.

Optional Post Draft

Zapier 这个 Codex 用法很具体：不是「帮我写点代码」，而是「帮我把散落的上下文整理成工程团队能直接执行的 Jira epic / ticket」。

他们提到以前可能要花几周搜集资料，现在 Codex 可以从不同知识源里拉上下文，几个小时内产出更完整的实施说明。

这个方向很值得关注：AI agent 真正进入工程流，不一定先从写代码开始，而是先吃掉那些高摩擦的上下文整理和任务定义工作。

notes.title: Codex is your BFF when things are everything, everywhere, all at once 💫
notes.excerpt: This short OpenAI clip frames Codex as more than a coding assistant: for Zapier, it becomes an operating layer that connects knowledge from remote-work tools and turns scattered co
notes.html:
Codex is your BFF when things are everything, everywhere, all at once 💫

What This Is About

This short OpenAI clip frames Codex as more than a coding assistant: for Zapier, it becomes an operating layer that connects knowledge from remote-work tools and turns scattered context into useful engineering outputs.

Important Concepts

  • Codex as an operating layer: the speaker describes it as infrastructure for modern engineering work, not just "AI for coding."
  • Remote-first context assembly: Zapier works across Slack, Google Docs, Coda, and other tools, creating fragmented knowledge that Codex can pull together.
  • Cross-source synthesis: Codex helps produce postmortems, incident-response material, and feature tickets from multiple information sources.

Learning Takeaways

  • The most valuable agent workflows may be the ones that connect existing work systems rather than only generating code.
  • For distributed teams, reducing context-gathering overhead can directly improve engineering throughput.
  • Codex's product story is partly about turning messy organizational knowledge into concrete deliverables.

Questions To Explore

  • Which internal sources should be connected first for a team: Slack, docs, tickets, incident logs, or code?
  • How should teams validate that generated postmortems and tickets are complete and accurate?
  • What permissions model keeps cross-tool synthesis useful without overexposing sensitive context?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • Slack, Google Docs, and Coda as examples of remote-work context sources.
  • Postmortems, incident response, and feature-ticket generation as practical Codex outputs.
  • Zapier as the customer example used in the clip.

Optional Post Draft

Codex 的一个很清晰的定位：不只是帮你写代码，而是把工程团队散落在 Slack、文档、Coda、代码和工单里的上下文拉到一起，生成可以直接推进工作的东西。

短片里 Zapier 的例子很典型：远程团队最痛的不是没有信息，而是信息分散。Codex 的价值就是把这些上下文变成 postmortem、incident response、feature ticket 这类具体产物。

notes.title: Codex for Sales Teams: Moving Faster to Solve Customer Problems
notes.excerpt: An OpenAI account director describes using Codex as a single workspace for delegating technical and analytical tasks. For sales teams, the main benefit is shortening the path from
notes.html:
Codex for Sales Teams: Moving Faster to Solve Customer Problems

What This Is About

An OpenAI account director describes using Codex as a single workspace for delegating technical and analytical tasks. For sales teams, the main benefit is shortening the path from a customer question or idea to useful data, a working demo, or a tangible solution.

Important Concepts

  • One pane of glass: Codex can coordinate multiple delegated tasks from a single interface.
  • Self-service analysis: Salespeople can retrieve customer-related numbers without always waiting for a data science team.
  • Rapid prototyping: Ideas can be turned into workable demos during the customer-solving process.
  • Customer-facing leverage: Less time spent searching for information creates more time for direct customer work.

Learning Takeaways

  • Giving sales teams controlled access to technical tools can reduce cross-team wait times from hours or days to minutes.
  • The most valuable automation targets are often the steps between a customer question and a concrete response.
  • Rapid demos help make ideas tangible, improving collaboration and learning with customers.
  • Codex can complement specialist teams by handling routine retrieval and prototyping, while experts focus on higher-complexity work.

Questions To Explore

  • What data access controls are required for safe self-service customer analysis?
  • Which sales tasks can be delegated reliably, and which still require data science or engineering review?
  • How should teams validate numbers and demos before sharing them with customers?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • Codex for delegated analysis, information retrieval, and demo creation
  • Customer-query data pulls as a self-service workflow
  • Workable demos as a tool for collaborative customer problem-solving

Optional Post Draft

Codex can give sales teams a much shorter path from customer question to tangible answer. Instead of waiting hours or days for routine data pulls, a seller can retrieve information, prototype a demo, and spend more time solving problems with the customer. The important shift is not replacing specialist teams; it is removing routine bottlenecks so everyone can focus on higher-value work.

notes.id: 2026-06-03-对话高通-智能体爆发-6g与physical-ai背后的大赢家.md
notes.file: 2026-06-03-对话高通-智能体爆发-6g与physical-ai背后的大赢家.md
notes.title: 对话高通：智能体爆发、6G与Physical AI背后的大赢家
notes.excerpt: 这期是硅谷 101 在高通“骁友会”现场对话高通全球副总裁徐晧，讨论 AI 从云端大模型走向端侧设备后的机会：AI 手机、AIPC、XR 眼镜、汽车、机器人、6G 和 Physical AI。核心判断是，高通的芯片、通信和端侧 AI 积累，让它有机会成为边缘 AI 生态的重要底层玩家。 今年看 AI，不能只看云端大模型。
notes.html:
对话高通：智能体爆发、6G与Physical AI背后的大赢家

What This Is About

这期是硅谷 101 在高通“骁友会”现场对话高通全球副总裁徐晧，讨论 AI 从云端大模型走向端侧设备后的机会：AI 手机、AIPC、XR 眼镜、汽车、机器人、6G 和 Physical AI。核心判断是，高通的芯片、通信和端侧 AI 积累，让它有机会成为边缘 AI 生态的重要底层玩家。

Important Concepts

  • 端侧 AI：把部分推理、感知、个性化记忆和交互能力放到手机、PC、车、眼镜、机器人等终端上，降低延迟并提升隐私和体验。
  • 模型小型化：通过蒸馏、量化、LoRA、MoE、内存/功耗优化，让更小模型在端侧承担更多任务。
  • 异构计算架构：CPU、GPU、NPU 分工协作，让不同任务跑在最合适的硬件单元上。
  • Agentic UI：手机或超级应用从“点 App”转向“说需求”，由智能体调用信息和服务完成任务。
  • Physical AI：AI 进入机器人、汽车、XR 等真实世界设备，需要感知、低延迟、运动控制和本地决策。
  • 6G 与通感一体化：未来网络既传输数据，也利用无线信号感知物理世界，为机器人、车、低空经济等提供环境理解。
  • Split rendering：XR 眼镜把部分渲染放在眼镜上，部分放在手机或更强终端上，以降低眼镜端功耗和重量。

Learning Takeaways

  • AI 的下一阶段不只是在云端卷最强模型，也在端侧寻找高频、低延迟、个性化的落地场景。
  • 端侧 AI 的关键约束是性能、功耗、内存、成本和散热，不同设备的平衡点完全不同。
  • 手机上的 AI agent 可能有两条路线：系统级全语音智能体，或能调用多服务的 super app。
  • 机器人和汽车比手机更强调低延迟、本地决策、传感器融合和算力调度；云端只适合承担长期规划或低时延要求较弱的任务。
  • XR 眼镜的瓶颈不是单一芯片性能，而是重量、电池、隐私、连接和应用生态一起决定的系统问题。
  • AIPC 的更强用例可能不是“炫技”，而是本地 coding、邮件/照片/视频处理和私密个人数据推理。
  • 真正丝滑的 Physical AI 体验还需要生态打通，例如车、车库、充电、外卖、家居设备和智能体之间的协作。

Questions To Explore

  • 哪些端侧 AI 任务最适合本地推理，哪些必须依赖云端或边缘云？
  • AI 手机会优先由操作系统级智能体跑出来，还是由 Google/微信/支付宝式 super app 跑出来？
  • 6G 的“AI 原生网络”会先在哪些场景体现商业价值：机器人、车、XR、低空经济，还是工业物联网？
  • 端侧个人记忆如何在好用、隐私保护和可解释之间取得平衡？
  • XR 眼镜的 killer app 会是翻译、导航、直播、记忆检索，还是工作场景提示？

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • Video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eAYx6CHstNw
  • Products/platforms mentioned: Snapdragon, 骁龙数字底盘, Snapdragon Ride, AR1, 跃龙 QCS8550, 双骁龙 8797, Honor Robot Phone, K1 机器人, 零跑 D19, 千问 S1 眼镜。
  • Techniques mentioned: distillation, quantization, LoRA adaptation, MoE, VLA, world model, TSN, split rendering, local personalized library.

Optional Post Draft

今年看 AI，不能只看云端大模型。

硅谷 101 这期对话高通很清楚地指出另一条线：端侧 AI 正在变成主战场。手机、PC、车、XR 眼镜、机器人，都需要把一部分智能放到本地，因为真实世界设备最怕延迟，也最受功耗、内存和成本限制。

所以端侧 AI 的关键不是简单把大模型塞进硬件，而是蒸馏、量化、NPU、异构计算、云端协同、个人本地记忆和通信网络一起优化。

Physical AI 真正跑起来时，赢家可能不只是模型公司，也会是那些掌握芯片、通信和设备生态底层能力的公司。

notes.title: It's time to fly | Codex
notes.excerpt: This is a short OpenAI Codex brand vignette rather than a technical demo. It frames coding with Codex as a launch sequence: the user stays behind after bedtime, enters “mission con
notes.html:
It's time to fly | Codex

What This Is About

This is a short OpenAI Codex brand vignette rather than a technical demo. It frames coding with Codex as a launch sequence: the user stays behind after bedtime, enters “mission control,” and starts building a companion app as if beginning a spaceflight.

Important Concepts

  • Codex as command-driven coding: the line “the code awaits your command” positions Codex as an interactive agent that responds to developer intent.
  • Creative metaphor: the launch sequence turns software building into exploration, risk, and discovery.
  • Companion app framing: the only concrete build prompt in the transcript is “Let’s build a companion app.”
  • Product emotion over workflow detail: the video sells energy, possibility, and agency rather than explaining features.

Learning Takeaways

  • Short product videos can communicate positioning without showing UI, especially when the product is already known.
  • The “mission control” metaphor makes the developer feel like an operator directing a powerful system, not a passive tool user.
  • Codex is framed less as autocomplete and more as a collaborator that can help start and carry a project.
  • The transcript does not provide implementation details, so any technical takeaway should stay high level.

Questions To Explore

  • What specific Codex workflow or launch was this video supporting?
  • How does OpenAI want users to distinguish Codex from IDE autocomplete, chat, and general-purpose agents?
  • What does “companion app” refer to here: an example prompt, a product direction, or just a narrative device?
  • Which parts of the Codex experience most benefit from cinematic positioning versus concrete demos?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • Video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bJcA23ckzcY
  • Product mentioned: Codex.

Optional Post Draft

OpenAI’s short Codex video is basically a launch metaphor for coding.

No feature walkthrough, no UI tour. Just “mission control,” “cleared for launch,” and “the code awaits your command.”

The positioning is interesting: Codex is not presented as autocomplete, but as a system you direct. The developer becomes the operator, and building software becomes a launch sequence into “the edge of all possibility.”

notes.id: 2026-06-01-拆解机器人-肉身-量产与供应链-空翻之后-它还要学会接住一片落叶-机器人特辑.md
notes.file: 2026-06-01-拆解机器人-肉身-量产与供应链-空翻之后-它还要学会接住一片落叶-机器人特辑.md
notes.title: 拆解机器人“肉身”、量产与供应链：空翻之后，它还要学会接住一片落叶｜机器人特辑
notes.excerpt: 这期硅谷101拆解人形机器人的“肉身”：骨架、关节、传感器、电气架构、电池和供应链。视频的核心观点是，机器人能空翻、跑半马并不等于已经接近实用，真正难的是在成本、重量、强度、精度、散热、耐久和量产一致性之间找到系统级平衡。 Title ideas:
notes.html:
拆解机器人“肉身”、量产与供应链：空翻之后，它还要学会接住一片落叶｜机器人特辑

What This Is About

这期硅谷101拆解人形机器人的“肉身”：骨架、关节、传感器、电气架构、电池和供应链。视频的核心观点是，机器人能空翻、跑半马并不等于已经接近实用，真正难的是在成本、重量、强度、精度、散热、耐久和量产一致性之间找到系统级平衡。

Important Concepts

  • 骨架材料: 从钢、铝合金到镁合金、钛合金，目标是在轻量化和抗冲击之间取平衡。
  • 执行器: 机器人关节的核心，包含电机、减速器或丝杠、编码器、驱动器和传感器，是成本最高、技术最密集的部分。
  • 减速器: 用速度换扭矩，常见类型包括行星、谐波和 RV 减速器，不同关节对精度和抗冲击要求不同。
  • 直线执行器: 类似肌肉的推拉结构，常依赖丝杠，载荷大但动态性能、成本和量产难度仍是挑战。
  • 小脑/大脑架构: 大脑负责高层规划和模型推理，小脑负责毫秒级运动控制和平衡反馈。
  • 量产一致性: 每台机器人的关节背隙、传感器零点、电机曲线都可能不同，必须靠标定和在线校准来稳定同一套算法。

Learning Takeaways

  • 人形机器人最难的不是“拼起来能动”，而是长期、稳定、低成本、批量地好用。
  • 供应链成熟正在加速机器人进化，因为汽车、手机、自动驾驶的供应商和经验可以迁移过来。
  • 执行器是量产降本的关键环节，约占整机成本的大头，自研与采购之间取决于公司规模、算法需求和成本目标。
  • 触觉仍处在早期阶段，三维触觉数据、材料耐磨性、漂移控制和操作模型结合都还没有完全成熟。
  • 下一个真正有意义的里程碑未必是更炫的空翻，而是“接住一片落叶”这种感知、预测、控制和触觉融合的基础能力。

Questions To Explore

  • 哪些执行器部件最可能先被中国供应链快速降本？
  • 机器人公司什么时候该自研关节，什么时候该采购标准件？
  • 触觉传感器的数据如何进入操作模型训练，而不是只作为硬件卖点？
  • 大脑和小脑芯片一体化会先发生在高端机器人还是量产消费机器人上？
  • 如何设计标定流程，让同一套算法稳定跑在不同批次、不同老化状态的机器人上？

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • 波士顿动力 Atlas: 从液压到电驱的代表案例。
  • 特斯拉 Optimus: 纯视觉路线、自研芯片和机器人系统集成案例。
  • 英伟达 Orin / Thor: 当前和未来机器人“大脑”芯片的重要选择。
  • STM32、i.MX RT、瑞萨 RZ: 常见“小脑”控制芯片系列。
  • 绿的谐波、双环传动、中大力德、三花智控、拓普、立讯精密、宁德时代: 机器人供应链中被提到的关键公司。

Optional Post Draft

Title ideas:

  • 机器人会空翻，不代表它已经会生活
  • 人形机器人最难的不是身体，是一致性
  • 接住一片落叶，可能比空翻更难

Draft:

这期讲机器人硬件，最有意思的判断是：空翻不是终点，接住一片落叶才更像里程碑。

因为空翻可以提前编排动作，难在关节爆发和抗冲击；但接住落叶需要实时感知、预测轨迹、控制手臂、处理触觉反馈，还要在毫厘之间完成动作。

机器人真正的难点不是把零件拼起来，而是在重量、强度、成本、散热、精度、耐久和量产一致性之间做系统工程。

能动，是第一步。稳定、便宜、批量地好用，才是产业化。

notes.title: 内存暴涨，谁在哭？谁在笑？
notes.excerpt: 这期小Lin说解释为什么 AI 浪潮会把内存行业推到聚光灯下。核心逻辑是：AI 芯片需要高带宽内存 HBM，HBM 挤占普通 DRAM 产能，导致内存价格大涨；三星、SK 海力士、美光等上游厂商因此成为赢家，而手机、电脑、汽车等下游产品承压。 Title ideas:
notes.html:
内存暴涨，谁在哭？谁在笑？

What This Is About

这期小Lin说解释为什么 AI 浪潮会把内存行业推到聚光灯下。核心逻辑是：AI 芯片需要高带宽内存 HBM，HBM 挤占普通 DRAM 产能，导致内存价格大涨；三星、SK 海力士、美光等上游厂商因此成为赢家，而手机、电脑、汽车等下游产品承压。

Important Concepts

  • DRAM: 临时性高速存储，断电后数据消失，是手机、电脑、服务器运行时的关键部件。
  • HBM: 高带宽内存，通过堆叠芯片并贴近 GPU/AI 芯片来缓解“内存墙”。
  • 内存墙: 算力变强后，数据喂给芯片的速度跟不上，内存带宽成为瓶颈。
  • 周期行业: 内存制造固定成本高、产品同质化强，历史上容易经历扩产、过剩、价格暴跌、淘汰整合的循环。
  • AI 需求挤出效应: HBM 利润高，厂商把产能转向 HBM，普通内存供应减少，价格上涨。
  • 韩国双雄: 三星和 SK 海力士在 DRAM/HBM 产业链中占据核心地位，韩国股市也被这两家公司明显牵引。

Learning Takeaways

  • AI 的影响并不只体现在模型和应用层，它会沿着硬件供应链传导到内存、封装、能源、散热等环节。
  • SK 海力士早年押注 HBM，看似短期不赚钱，但在 AI 爆发后变成关键战略优势。
  • HBM 改变了 DRAM 的商业属性：从高度同质化、强周期，逐步走向定制化、高附加值、客户绑定更深的模式。
  • 上游供给瓶颈会把成本压力传导给普通消费者，最终表现为手机、电脑、汽车等产品涨价。
  • 观察 AI 产业链时，真正稀缺的环节往往会从“算力本身”扩散到“谁能喂饱算力”。

Questions To Explore

  • HBM4 之后，三星、SK 海力士、美光的竞争格局会不会再次洗牌？
  • 台积电 CoWoS 等先进封装在 HBM 产业链中到底有多关键？
  • 普通 DRAM 价格上涨会如何影响消费电子和汽车供应链？
  • 中国厂商在 DRAM、HBM、先进封装上分别卡在哪些环节？
  • 如果 AI 需求放缓，HBM 会不会重新变成强周期行业的一部分？

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • SK 海力士: HBM 早期重仓者，受益于英伟达 H100 等 AI GPU 需求。
  • 三星: 传统 DRAM 巨头，也在追赶 HBM 和 AI 芯片客户。
  • 美光: 美国内存厂商，逐步把资源转向 AI 高端内存市场。
  • HBM3/HBM4: AI 芯片时代的关键内存标准。
  • 英伟达 H100: 推动 HBM 需求爆发的代表性 AI GPU。

Optional Post Draft

Title ideas:

  • AI 涨价，最后可能体现在你的内存条上
  • HBM：AI 时代真正被“喂饱”的生意
  • 为什么 SK 海力士突然这么猛？

Draft:

这期讲内存涨价，最有意思的点不是“AI 很火”，而是 AI 怎么沿着供应链一路传导。

AI 芯片需要 HBM。HBM 利润高、需求猛，于是三星、SK 海力士、美光把更多产能转过去。结果普通 DRAM 被挤出，手机、电脑、汽车这些下游产品都开始感受到价格压力。

SK 海力士的故事也很典型：一个早年看起来小众、贵、不赚钱的技术押注，在 AI 爆发后突然变成战略高地。

AI 的下一个瓶颈，不一定在模型，可能在内存、封装、能源这些更“硬”的地方。

notes.title: 对话教育大咖：当AI开始重新定义“能力”，教育还会一样吗？
notes.excerpt: 这期硅谷101讨论 AI 如何重新定义教育中的“能力”。节目采访教育从业者和硅谷教育人物，核心问题是：当知识获取和标准化任务越来越容易被 AI 接管，孩子还应该学什么，学校和家长又该如何培养下一代。 Title ideas:
notes.html:
对话教育大咖：当AI开始重新定义“能力”，教育还会一样吗？

What This Is About

这期硅谷101讨论 AI 如何重新定义教育中的“能力”。节目采访教育从业者和硅谷教育人物，核心问题是：当知识获取和标准化任务越来越容易被 AI 接管，孩子还应该学什么，学校和家长又该如何培养下一代。

Important Concepts

  • 能力优先: 知识不再是唯一目标，元认知、判断力、跨领域连接、问题拆解和表达能力更重要。
  • Becoming human: 教育目标不是让人更像机器，而是发展人的好奇心、动机、创造力和非理性但珍贵的人性部分。
  • AI 与学习范式: AI 可能融合家庭自然学习、师徒式学习和学校式学习，而不是只提高课堂效率。
  • 课程重构: 从学科边界清晰、统一进度，转向真实问题导向、个性化路径和能力地图。
  • Builder 视角: 让孩子把 AI 当作创造工具，而不是单纯外包答案的机器。
  • Magic Moment: 捕捉孩子举手、表达、解决问题等高光时刻，给到正向反馈。

Learning Takeaways

  • AI 时代教育的关键不是“还要不要学知识”，而是知识如何服务于能力和创造。
  • 阅读仍然重要，因为 AI 可以总结，但深度阅读训练的是理解、吸收和判断。
  • 家长和学校的角色会从安排路线、传递知识，转向提供真实社群、关注、鼓励和高质量反馈。
  • 对孩子来说，AI 使用边界取决于身份定位：如果是被考问者，AI 容易变成作弊工具；如果是 builder，AI 会变成放大创造力的工具。
  • 早期教育值得重视逻辑、表达、抽象、审美、问题解决，而不是过早切成单一学科训练。

Questions To Explore

  • 怎么设计一个让孩子“用 AI 创造”而不是“用 AI 交答案”的学习任务？
  • AI 个性化学习和学校社群体验之间如何平衡？
  • 家长如何观察并反馈孩子的 Magic Moment，而不是只盯成绩？
  • STEM 学习在 AI 时代应如何从技能训练转向底层思维训练？
  • 未来的课程体系应该如何衡量元认知、判断力和创造力？

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • Think Academy / Think Kids: 节目中提到的好未来海外教育品牌和早期儿童思维课程。
  • Howard Gardner: 关于多元智能和学习方式的教育研究者。
  • Esther Wojcicki: 硅谷教育人物，强调自由探索、独立思考和阅读。
  • CPAE/CPA 思路: 从具象、图像到抽象，再加入表达，帮助孩子建立思维过程。
  • AI-assisted reading: 让 AI 辅助阅读，但不替代阅读能力本身。

Optional Post Draft

Title ideas:

  • AI 时代，孩子最该学的可能不是答案
  • 教育的目标要从知识转向能力
  • 不要把孩子培养成 AI 的外包经理

Draft:

这期关于 AI 和教育，我最喜欢的一个区分是：孩子是在“回答问题”，还是在“创造东西”。

如果学习场景只是考问，AI 很容易变成外包答案的工具。

但如果孩子被定位成 builder，AI 就会变成创造力的放大器。

所以 AI 时代的教育重点，可能不是让孩子记住更多知识，而是培养：

  • 元认知
  • 判断力
  • 跨领域连接
  • 问题拆解
  • 表达能力
  • 对真实世界的好奇心

知识仍然重要，但它越来越像手段。能力才是目标。

notes.title: Terence Tao on How AI Is Changing Mathematics
notes.excerpt: Terence Tao reflects on how AI is changing mathematical work. The clip frames AI as a way to reduce "cognitive friction": mathematicians can explore more speculative ideas, offload
notes.html:
Terence Tao on How AI Is Changing Mathematics

What This Is About

Terence Tao reflects on how AI is changing mathematical work. The clip frames AI as a way to reduce "cognitive friction": mathematicians can explore more speculative ideas, offload tedious computations, search literature more effectively, and share not just final results but the paths they used to get there.

Important Concepts

  • Cognitive friction: the hidden cost of every intellectual task that requires attention, memory, search, or calculation.
  • AI-assisted mathematics: using AI as a collaborator for literature search, computation, experimentation, and project acceleration.
  • Exploration bandwidth: AI lets researchers try more "crazy" or speculative paths because the cost of checking them is lower.
  • Process transparency: Tao hopes people share the routes, attempts, and tools behind results, not only the polished final product.
  • Frontier science automation: OpenAI frames the goal as enabling many scientists and mathematicians to do breakthrough work, not merely having AI win individual prizes.

Learning Takeaways

  • AI's near-term value in mathematics may be less about replacing deep insight and more about removing routine friction around search, computation, and verification.
  • Lowering the cost of failed attempts changes behavior: researchers can afford to test more ideas and collaborate across more projects.
  • Capturing intermediate reasoning paths could become a new kind of research artifact, useful for learning and reproducibility.
  • The strongest AI workflows may combine human taste and direction with machine speed on tedious or high-branching tasks.

Questions To Explore

  • What parts of mathematical research are easiest to accelerate today: literature search, symbolic computation, proof checking, conjecture generation, or exposition?
  • How should researchers document AI-assisted paths so others can audit or learn from them?
  • What does "prime time" AI-assisted mathematics look like in daily practice?
  • Could this workflow apply to other high-friction knowledge work, like technical writing or product research?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • IPAM: Institute for Pure and Applied Mathematics, where Tao is director of special projects.
  • AI literature search: useful for finding related work and avoiding duplicated effort.
  • AI computation assistant: helpful for finishing tedious calculations that interrupt conceptual flow.
  • Research process logs: a potential format for sharing prompts, dead ends, alternate paths, and verification steps.

Optional Post Draft

Title ideas:

  • Terence Tao's useful frame for AI: cognitive friction
  • AI may change math by making failed attempts cheaper
  • The future research artifact might be the path, not just the result

Draft:

Terence Tao's point about AI and mathematics is sharper than "AI will solve math."

The useful frame is cognitive friction.

Every search, calculation, check, and tedious branch has a cost. When AI lowers that cost, researchers can try stranger ideas, move across more projects, and spend more attention on judgment instead of mechanical work.

The part I liked most: Tao hopes people share the paths they used to reach results, not just the final product. In an AI-assisted world, the route may become as valuable as the answer.

notes.title: Windows Computer Use and mobile access for Codex
notes.excerpt: OpenAI is expanding the Codex Windows app with Computer Use and mobile access. The update lets Codex operate local desktop apps, start or monitor tasks from mobile, and continue wo
notes.html:
Windows Computer Use and mobile access for Codex

What This Is About

OpenAI is expanding the Codex Windows app with Computer Use and mobile access. The update lets Codex operate local desktop apps, start or monitor tasks from mobile, and continue work while the user is away from the desk.

Important Concepts

  • Computer Use lets Codex control installed desktop apps, including the screen and cursor, for tasks that cannot be handled purely in the browser.
  • Codex for Chrome is still the better fit for browser-only work because it can operate across multiple tabs in the background.
  • Computer Use is enabled in the Codex app settings, then added to a conversation from the composer with "Add computer."
  • Users can @mention specific installed apps when asking Codex to perform local computer tasks.
  • Mobile access connects the Windows Codex app with the ChatGPT iOS or Android app through a QR-code setup flow.

Learning Takeaways

  • Choose the execution surface based on the job: browser integration for web tasks, Computer Use for local app workflows.
  • Computer Use changes the collaboration pattern because Codex actively controls the machine, so the user can step away while work continues.
  • Mobile access turns Codex into a more persistent agent workflow: users can check running tasks or start new ones while away, as long as the Windows computer stays on and online.
  • The feature depends on account linkage and device availability, not just app installation.

Questions To Explore

  • What guardrails exist when Codex controls the desktop, especially for sensitive apps or destructive actions?
  • How well does Computer Use handle multi-step workflows across native apps compared with browser-based automation?
  • Can mobile-started tasks use the same plugins and local context as desktop-started tasks?
  • What visibility or approval controls are available while Codex is using the screen and cursor?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • Codex app for Windows: enable Computer Use in Settings, then use "Add computer" in a new conversation.
  • Codex for Chrome: preferred for background browser tasks across multiple tabs.
  • ChatGPT mobile app on iOS or Android: used to connect to Codex, monitor running tasks, and start new work.
  • Setup path mentioned: Codex Settings -> Connections, then scan the QR code from mobile.

Optional Post Draft

Codex on Windows is moving from "chat about work" toward "operate the machine." The new Computer Use mode can control local desktop apps, while mobile access lets you monitor or start tasks from the ChatGPT app when your computer is still on and online.

The practical split is useful: use Codex for Chrome for browser-only work, and use Computer Use when the workflow needs installed apps or the desktop itself. The big question to test next is how much control and visibility users get while Codex is moving the cursor and working across apps.

notes.title: These Models are Crazy!

notes.html:
These Models are Crazy!

What This Is About

This short clip highlights how quickly AI coding tools such as Codex are changing developer productivity. The main claim is that work which previously took teams weeks or months can now sometimes be completed in minutes or hours.

Important Concepts

  • Rapid model improvement is directly affecting software development workflows.
  • Developer productivity gains are being observed by engineering leaders in day-to-day work.
  • Codex is used as an example of tools that compress the time needed for complex engineering tasks.

Learning Takeaways

  • The perceived value of AI coding tools is not only code generation, but cycle-time reduction across engineering work.
  • Leadership teams are noticing qualitative changes in how fast work moves.
  • The strongest learning signal from the clip is the magnitude of time compression: minutes or hours versus weeks or months.

Questions To Explore

  • Which categories of engineering work are seeing the largest time savings?
  • Are these productivity gains mostly from implementation, debugging, research, coordination, or review?
  • What changes in process are needed when individual contributors can complete formerly team-sized tasks?
  • How should teams measure real productivity gains without confusing speed with quality?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • Codex is the named example of a tool driving rapid developer productivity gains.
  • Slack messages among engineering leadership are used as an anecdotal signal that the change is visible inside teams.

Optional Post Draft

One useful way to frame AI coding tools: they are not just "faster autocomplete." The bigger shift is cycle-time compression. In this clip, the claim is that tasks once measured in team-weeks or team-months are starting to happen in individual minutes or hours.

That raises a better question than "can AI write code?": which engineering bottlenecks disappear first, and which new review, quality, and coordination habits need to replace them?

notes.title: Loblaw Ships Faster with Codex
notes.excerpt: This OpenAI customer clip shows how Loblaw uses ChatGPT Enterprise and Codex across a large retail organization. The focus is speed: work that used to take teams weeks or months ca
notes.html:
Loblaw Ships Faster with Codex

What This Is About

This OpenAI customer clip shows how Loblaw uses ChatGPT Enterprise and Codex across a large retail organization. The focus is speed: work that used to take teams weeks or months can now happen in minutes or hours, especially across engineering, e-commerce, product imagery, and consumer-facing app experiments.

Important Concepts

  • ChatGPT Enterprise rollout: Loblaw has ChatGPT Enterprise in the hands of every colleague.
  • Codex productivity: Codex is framed as a tool that materially compresses engineering work cycles.
  • Multi-business retail context: Loblaw spans grocery, pharmacy, beauty, apparel, e-commerce, and consumer apps.
  • First-party platform AI: The company is building chat experiences on its own platform.
  • Creative asset expansion: AI makes it cheaper to generate many more product presentation variants, such as shoes shown in different scenarios.
  • Organizational excitement: Engineering leaders are reacting to model capability jumps as a practical productivity shift, not just a demo.

Learning Takeaways

  • AI adoption in large companies often starts by putting tools broadly into employees' hands, then letting teams discover high-leverage workflows.
  • Codex's value is not only writing code; it compresses coordination and implementation time for engineering teams.
  • Retail is a useful AI case study because it combines software, merchandising, customer experience, product imagery, and operations.
  • Generative tools can turn previously expensive creative variation into a normal part of the workflow.
  • The phrase "minutes and hours instead of weeks and months" is a good test: the strongest AI use cases collapse a real bottleneck, not just add novelty.

Questions To Explore

  • Which Loblaw workflows were most improved by Codex versus general ChatGPT Enterprise use?
  • How does a large retailer govern AI use across engineering, creative, and customer-facing product teams?
  • What quality review process catches mistakes in generated product imagery or app code?
  • How do teams measure the productivity gains from Codex in real shipping cycles?
  • Could this Daily Digest app use a similar pattern: broad AI tooling plus narrow workflow-specific automation?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • Loblaw: Canadian retail group with grocery, pharmacy, apparel, beauty, and e-commerce businesses.
  • ChatGPT Enterprise: deployed across colleagues in the organization.
  • Codex: cited as a major driver of developer productivity.
  • PC Express app: Loblaw's Canadian grocery/e-commerce app mentioned in the clip.
  • AI product imagery: example of multiplying product shots across scenarios and models.

Optional Post Draft

Title ideas:

  • The useful AI metric: weeks to hours
  • Loblaw's AI lesson is workflow compression
  • Retail AI is not just chatbots

Draft:

The Loblaw clip is short, but the useful signal is clear: AI matters most when it collapses real workflow time.

The claim that stood out: work that used to take teams weeks or months can now happen in minutes or hours.

That shows up in engineering with Codex, but also in retail-specific work like product imagery, e-commerce experiences, and customer apps.

The pattern feels general: give teams strong tools broadly, then look for bottlenecks where AI turns expensive variation or slow implementation into something cheap enough to do every day.

notes.title: Builders Unscripted: Ep. 3 - Matias Castello, Product Lead at Alchemy
notes.excerpt: This Builders Unscripted episode follows Matias Castello, product lead at Alchemy, and how he uses Codex and GPT-5.5 across work and personal projects. The conversation covers AI-a
notes.html:
Builders Unscripted: Ep. 3 - Matias Castello, Product Lead at Alchemy

What This Is About

This Builders Unscripted episode follows Matias Castello, product lead at Alchemy, and how he uses Codex and GPT-5.5 across work and personal projects. The conversation covers AI-assisted docs edits, retroactive incident review, internal PM skills, agent-friendly developer platforms, Codex-driven feature experiments, and playful custom interfaces like Discord, iPhone, and Apple Watch entry points for starting coding jobs.

Important Concepts

  • Codex as teammate: Alchemy uses Codex for docs edits, code review, PR iteration, PM work, and internal skills.
  • Retroactive code review: teams can replay old incidents through Codex to see whether AI review would have caught the issue.
  • Shared skill repositories: Alchemy uses reusable skills for PRDs, customer feedback analysis, and other product workflows.
  • Agent-aware developer platforms: developer tooling now needs to serve humans using AI and, increasingly, autonomous agents directly.
  • Preference files and upfront clarification: Matias reduces bad surprises by encoding how he likes to work and forcing assumptions into the open.
  • Feature-flagged experiments: Codex researches, designs, and builds candidate features behind toggles so a human can quickly choose what stays.
  • Codex App Server interfaces: Matias extends Codex through a Mac writing app, Discord, iPhone, and Apple Watch voice workflows.

Learning Takeaways

  • Code review can be the adoption wedge for AI inside serious engineering teams because it connects directly to incident prevention.
  • The highest-leverage Codex workflows are not one-off prompts; they are repeatable processes encoded as skills, project conventions, and preference files.
  • Good delegation requires giving the agent enough product taste, constraints, and decision criteria before it starts building.
  • Feature flags are a practical pattern for agent-built work: let the agent generate options, but keep human review cheap and reversible.
  • AI changes platform design. If developers build with agents, APIs, docs, onboarding, and signup flows need to be legible to agents too.
  • Personal productivity can shift from "sit and build" to "dispatch work, review results, and keep control at decision points."

Questions To Explore

  • What would a strong AGENTS.md or preference file look like for my own Daily Digest workflow?
  • Which repeated PM or learning tasks should become reusable Codex skills?
  • Could daily notes support feature-flag-style experiments, where drafts, post formats, or summaries are generated as selectable variants?
  • What parts of this app's docs or workflows would fail if an autonomous agent tried to use them?
  • How should agent-generated feature ideas be scored before implementation?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • Codex in Slack: used for lightweight documentation edits without running the site locally.
  • Codex review: used to review PRs and replay prior incidents.
  • Linear: used as the project management interface where Codex can create milestones, issues, and execution plans.
  • Internal skills repo: shared source of reusable workflows for PM and engineering tasks.
  • Feature flags: used to test agent-built feature experiments independently.
  • Codex App Server: used to build custom entry points around a ChatGPT/Codex account.
  • OpenClaw, Discord, iPhone, and Apple Watch integrations: examples of alternative interfaces for dispatching coding jobs.

Optional Post Draft

Title ideas:

  • The real Codex unlock is workflow design
  • Matias Castello's agent workflow: dispatch, review, decide
  • AI builders need skills, preferences, and feature flags

Draft:

Matias Castello's Codex setup has a useful lesson: the magic is not one prompt.

It is the system around the prompt:

  • a preference file that explains how he likes to work
  • reusable skills for repeated workflows
  • Linear as the planning surface
  • Codex doing research before implementation
  • feature flags so generated ideas are easy to test
  • phone, Discord, and watch entry points for dispatching jobs

The pattern is simple but powerful: let the agent generate and build options, then keep the human at the decision points.

That feels like a much more realistic model of AI-assisted building than "the model does everything."

notes.title: R&D Part 1
notes.excerpt: This OpenAI R&D clip follows OpenAI's collaboration with Chip Ganassi Racing. The theme is using AI to connect a race team's scattered historical, telemetry, strategy, training, an
notes.html:
R&D Part 1

What This Is About

This OpenAI R&D clip follows OpenAI's collaboration with Chip Ganassi Racing. The theme is using AI to connect a race team's scattered historical, telemetry, strategy, training, and pit-stop data so decisions can happen faster during tiny windows where tenths of a second matter.

Important Concepts

  • Data integration: The team has historical race data, test-session data, live race data, competitor data, and human-performance data that are hard to process manually.
  • Between-session learning: Race teams have only hours between sessions to turn observations into setup and strategy changes.
  • Strategy under uncertainty: The fastest car does not always win; pit timing, fuel, traffic, and competitor behavior can change the race.
  • Human performance support: ChatGPT is used as an assistant strength and conditioning coach for pit crew training.
  • Timing stand signal filtering: Engineers must decide which information is important enough to pass to the driver in real time.
  • Trust and execution: During a pit sequence, the driver and crew rely on precise instructions such as exact fueling duration.

Learning Takeaways

  • AI is valuable in racing because the environment is data-rich, time-constrained, and brutally sensitive to small gains.
  • The strongest use case is not replacing experts; it is helping experts search more data, compare more scenarios, and act faster.
  • Racing is a useful analogy for operations work: preparation dominates, but live decisions still need fast synthesis.
  • ChatGPT can help both technical workflows and human workflows, from race strategy analysis to workout planning.
  • Real adoption happens when model outputs translate into measurable efficiency gains outside the lab.

Questions To Explore

  • Which racing datasets are most useful to connect first: telemetry, pit stops, competitor strategy, weather, or driver feedback?
  • How does the team validate AI-generated race strategy before using it under pressure?
  • What parts of the timing stand workflow should be automated versus kept human-controlled?
  • Could a similar AI system help other high-pressure operations teams prepare and adapt between short cycles?
  • How should teams measure "tenths of a second" gains from AI when many race variables change at once?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • Chip Ganassi Racing: OpenAI's motorsports collaboration partner in the video.
  • Long Beach Grand Prix: The race context used to show preparation, strategy, pit timing, and live decisions.
  • ChatGPT: Used for analysis support and human-performance workout planning.
  • Pit stop workflow: A concrete example where seconds, fuel duration, traffic, and trust all intersect.
  • Historical race analysis: Comparing prior years and competitor patterns to improve current strategy.

Optional Post Draft

Title ideas:

  • AI's best racing use case is faster synthesis
  • Tenths of a second are a data problem
  • Racing shows where AI becomes operational

Draft:

The OpenAI x Chip Ganassi clip is a clean example of AI in a high-pressure workflow.

The team is not short on data. It has historical races, test sessions, telemetry, strategy notes, pit data, competitor behavior, and human-performance inputs. The hard part is connecting it quickly enough to matter.

That is why racing is such a good AI case study: a tenth of a second can change the outcome, but the decision still has to be trusted by humans in the loop.

The pattern generalizes well: AI is most useful when it helps experts search more context, compare more scenarios, and act inside a narrow decision window.

notes.title: How Abridge uses GPT-5.5 for clinical decision support
notes.excerpt: This short OpenAI customer clip explains how Abridge uses GPT-5.5 for clinical decision support. The core idea is that better reasoning and tool use let the system synthesize medic
notes.html:
How Abridge uses GPT-5.5 for clinical decision support

What This Is About

This short OpenAI customer clip explains how Abridge uses GPT-5.5 for clinical decision support. The core idea is that better reasoning and tool use let the system synthesize medical knowledge, patient context, and live doctor-patient conversation into denser information at the point of care, while leaving the final decision with clinicians.

Important Concepts

  • Clinical decision support: AI is used to help clinicians reason through patient care, not to replace the clinician's final judgment.
  • Tool use: Abridge reports that GPT-5.5 performed better as more tools were added, suggesting stronger synthesis across information sources.
  • Information density: The product goal is to surface the highest-value medical context at the moment a clinician needs it.
  • Patient context: Useful clinical AI needs more than generic medical knowledge; it also needs the patient's record and live conversation context.
  • Evaluation set: Abridge measured model behavior on its own clinical evaluation set, where it saw improved reasoning and tool use.

Learning Takeaways

  • In high-stakes domains, the value of a model is not just raw answer quality; it is whether it can use tools and context reliably under real workflow constraints.
  • Medicine is a good example of why context windows, retrieval, and tool orchestration matter. The model has to combine many dense sources without losing rigor.
  • The clip frames AI as a decision-support layer, which is an important product positioning choice for healthcare.
  • The most interesting claim is that performance improved as tool count increased. That suggests the model may handle complex tool ecosystems better than previous generations.

Questions To Explore

  • What kinds of tools did Abridge add: guideline search, EHR retrieval, medication databases, lab interpretation, or note review?
  • How does Abridge evaluate clinical reasoning quality and safety?
  • What guardrails ensure that clinicians understand evidence, uncertainty, and source provenance?
  • How does the system handle contradictory patient context or outdated medical information?
  • Where is the boundary between "decision support" and making a clinical recommendation?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • Abridge: healthcare AI company focused on clinical documentation and decision support.
  • GPT-5.5: the model mentioned in the clip as improving clinical reasoning and tool use.
  • Point-of-care workflows: a useful lens for evaluating whether AI output is timely and actionable.
  • Clinical evaluation sets: worth studying as a pattern for testing AI systems in specialized domains.

Optional Post Draft

Title ideas:

  • Why healthcare AI is really about context
  • Abridge + GPT-5.5: clinical AI as decision support
  • The interesting part is not the answer, it is tool use

Draft:

Abridge's use case is a good reminder that healthcare AI is not just "ask a model a medical question."

The hard part is synthesis: medical knowledge + patient context + live doctor-patient conversation + tools, all at the point of care.

The most interesting signal from the clip: as Abridge added more tools, GPT-5.5 performed better on their evaluation set. That matters because real healthcare workflows are tool-heavy and context-heavy.

The positioning is also careful: AI supports clinicians, but clinicians still make the final decision.

Gemini的失落：Google越来越不敢冒险了 | 专访Gemini前核心科学家Andrew Dai

What This Is About

这段转录内容来自对 Gemini 前核心科学家 Andrew Dai 的访谈片段，重点讨论 Gemini 2.x 到 3.0 的进展、数据质量对大模型能力的影响，以及 Google 在大规模预训练项目中越来越保守的组织约束。可用 transcript 很短，下面笔记只基于当前文本。

Important Concepts

  • 数据质量: Andrew 认为大模型能力差距很大程度来自数据，关键是筛掉 spam 和无用数据，让模型真正学到新东西。
  • Gemini 2.0: 被描述为一次重要翻身仗，方法更成熟，包括 MoE 和长上下文等方向。
  • Gemini 2.5: 访谈中提到 Noam Shazeer 从 OpenAI 回到 Google 后对模型改进有帮助。
  • Gemini 3.0: 通过新的数据方法和改进短暂超过 GPT，引发市场对 Google 回归的期待。
  • 大组织保守性: 当项目有几千人和大量 GPU 投入时，团队更难采用激进但高风险的新想法。
  • 创业动机: Andrew 离开的原因之一，是觉得在大公司内的 progress 和方法变得相对保守。

Learning Takeaways

  • 模型进步不只是架构问题，数据选择、清洗、质量检测可能同样决定上限。
  • 大公司在资源和人才上有优势，但超大规模项目会天然压低试错风险偏好。
  • 当预训练成本极高时，组织会更倾向于可解释、可控、低风险的改进路线。
  • “短暂领先”不等于长期优势，模型竞争会快速回到数据、组织速度和冒险能力的综合比拼。
  • 对前沿 AI 团队来说，技术路线和组织结构很难分开看。

Questions To Explore

  • Gemini 2.0 到 3.0 的数据质量改进具体包括哪些方法？
  • MoE 和长上下文在 Gemini 系列中分别解决了什么瓶颈？
  • 大模型团队如何在巨额训练成本和激进实验之间做风险管理？
  • Google 的组织结构是否天然不利于高风险前沿模型试验？
  • 创业团队在哪些模型改进方向上比大公司更容易冒险？

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • Gemini 2.0 / 2.5 / 3.0: 访谈中讨论的 Google 模型迭代节点。
  • MoE: 用于提升模型容量和效率的重要架构方向。
  • Long context: 长上下文能力被提到为模型成熟方向之一。
  • Noam Shazeer: 访谈中提到其回归 Google 对 Gemini 2.5 有帮助。
  • Data filtering / data quality checks: 访谈中最核心的模型改进杠杆。

Optional Post Draft

Title ideas:

  • 大模型竞争，数据可能比架构更关键
  • Google 的问题也许不是没人才
  • 前沿模型里的“保守”成本

Draft:

这段 Andrew Dai 的访谈片段里，最值得记的是一句判断：大模型差距很大程度在数据。

不是网上数据越多越好，而是要找到真正能让模型学到新东西的数据，过滤掉 spam 和无效内容，并且更严格地检测质量。

另一个点是组织风险。Google 有人才、有 GPU、有基础设施，但当一个项目牵涉几千人和巨大预训练成本时，团队会更倾向于保守路线，很难押注特别激进的新想法。

所以前沿模型竞争不只是技术竞赛，也是组织冒险能力的竞赛。

notes.html:
Build Hour: Agents SDK

What This Is About

This OpenAI Build Hour introduces recent updates to the Agents SDK and shows how to build a sandbox-using agent. The session explains why production agents are hard, how the SDK borrows from Codex-style long-running work, and how sandboxes, skills, hosted shell, containers, memory, and session management help agents do real work across files and tools.

Important Concepts

  • Agents SDK: an open-source framework for building agent loops with tools, orchestration, and model-native work patterns.
  • Codex-style harness: an agent loop inspired by Codex, including shell interaction, long-running command handling, file editing, context compaction, and computer use patterns.
  • Sandbox-using agents: agents can work in isolated environments with files, shell commands, and controlled execution.
  • Harness vs compute: the session emphasizes separating the agent orchestration layer from ephemeral sandbox compute so state can be rehydrated if a container dies.
  • Hosted shell tool: a Responses API feature that spins up a container, lets the model work on files, and returns results.
  • Skills: reusable bundles of instructions, scripts, and resources that specialize an agent for a task.
  • Capabilities: SDK objects that can combine tools, instructions, manifests, and startup behavior.
  • Session management: the SDK handles snapshotting and resuming tasks so agents can work over long trajectories.

Learning Takeaways

  • Production agents are not just "LLM + tool loop." You need sandbox lifecycle, state recovery, file access, security boundaries, and a way to add domain-specific context.
  • Separating harness from compute is a strong architecture pattern. The sandbox can be ephemeral, while orchestration and state live elsewhere.
  • Skills are useful because they make domain expertise reusable and versionable. GitHub or the Skills API can become the source of truth.
  • The SDK is trying to move developers away from writing orchestration plumbing and toward building product-specific behavior.
  • Long-running agents need context compaction and asynchronous shell handling; otherwise they get stuck on real-world tasks that take time.
  • The demo's task-tracker example shows a practical product pattern: assign work to an agent, upload files, let it inspect/edit them in a sandbox, then return a result.

Questions To Explore

  • When should I use hosted shell directly versus the full Agents SDK?
  • What is the cleanest way to store and version skills for a personal or team workflow?
  • How should secrets be handled when the agent is using an external sandbox?
  • Which sandbox backend is best for a small app: local Docker, Modal, E2B, Cloudflare, Vercel, or another provider?
  • How does session rehydration work in practice when files have changed or a task spans many hours?
  • Could this daily learning app use a skill that defines my preferred note style and post style?

Useful Examples, Tools, Or Links

  • OpenAI Agents SDK: the main framework discussed in the session.
  • Responses API hosted shell: lightweight container-backed execution for file-based tasks.
  • Skills API: central storage/versioning for reusable agent skills.
  • Docker sandbox: local development option shown in the demo.
  • GitHub skill repositories: useful for versioning and reviewing changes to skills.
  • Agentic task tracker demo: example pattern for assigning work to agents with uploaded files.

Optional Post Draft

Title ideas:

  • Agents are becoming less like chatbots and more like workers
  • The interesting part of Agents SDK is the sandbox
  • Why production agents need more than a tool loop

Draft:

The OpenAI Build Hour on Agents SDK made one thing clear: building useful agents is no longer just about calling an LLM in a loop.

The hard parts are more practical:

  • where the agent works
  • how it edits files
  • how it survives long tasks
  • how you isolate compute
  • how you add reusable domain knowledge
  • how you resume after a sandbox dies

The architecture I liked most: split the harness from the compute. Let the sandbox be disposable, but keep orchestration and state somewhere durable.

That feels like the difference between a demo agent and something you can actually put into a product.
